questions.questionText: If India introduces horizontal reservation for transgender persons within existing SC/ST/ OBC quotas to address their intersectional vulnerabilities. This policy best represents:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The introduction of horizontal reservation for transgender persons within existing SC/ST/OBC quotas aims to address their intersectional disadvantages. This directly reflects the idea of **distributive justice**,
which is concerned with the **fair distribution of social goods and opportunities** such as education, jobs and political representation.
● **Distributive justice** refers to the fair allocation of **social goods and opportunities** such as education, employment, political power and welfare. Modern democratic societies recognise that simply treating everyone equally is not enough when people begin from **unequal social positions**.
● Justice, therefore, requires not only equal rights but also **special measures for those who are structurally** **disadvantaged**, so that they can enjoy real and effective equality.
● The idea of **intersectionality** highlights that disadvantage is often **multi-layered**. A person may face discrimination not only because of caste or class but also because of **gender identity, disability or** **sexuality**.
● Transgender persons, for example, may belong to SC, ST, OBC or General categories, yet they experience **additional stigma, social exclusion and economic vulnerability** that ordinary vertical reservations do not adequately address.
● **Retributive justice** is the traditional model of justice that focuses on the **punishment of the offender for** **wrongdoing**. **Restorative justice** is a philosophy of justice that focuses on **healing, rehabilitation and** **reintegration of victims**, rather than only punishing offenders.
● **Commutative justice** is a principle that focuses on **fairness in agreements and exchanges** between individuals. It emphasizes what is owed between parties, particularly in business transactions. This form of justice seeks to ensure that all parties receive what they are entitled to, fostering trust and integrity in personal and commercial relationships.
● **Procedural justice** speaks to the idea of fair processes, and how **people’s perception of fairness is** **strongly impacted by the quality of their experiences** and not only the end result of these experiences. Procedural justice theory has been applied to various settings, including supervisor-employee relations within organizations, educational settings, and the criminal justice system. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Tribunals in India:
I. - Tribunals constituted under Article 323B are confined to the subject matters expressly enumerated in that Article.
II. - The secretarial staff of both Houses of Parliament comes under the jurisdiction of the Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT).
III. Foreigners Tribunals are quasi-judicial bodies
constituted by the Ministry of External Affairs.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Provisions related to the establishment of Tribunals were introduced through the **42nd Constitutional** **Amendment Act, 1976,** which added a new **Part XIV-A** titled “Tribunals”, consisting of **Article 323A** (Administrative Tribunals) and **Article 323B** (Tribunals for other matters).
● The Supreme Court of India has affirmed that the **list of subjects in Article 323B** of the Constitution of India is **illustrative, not exhaustive**, allowing Parliament and State Legislatures to establish tribunals for other matters falling within their legislative powers under the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India.  **So, statement I is not correct**
● The **Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT)** has jurisdiction over All-India Services, Central Civil Services, civil posts under the Centre and civilian employees of defence services. However, **members of** **the defence forces, officers and servants of the Supreme Court, and the secretarial staff of** **Parliament are expressly excluded** from its jurisdiction. **So,** **statement II is not correct**.
● **Foreigners Tribunals** are constituted under the **Foreigners (Tribunals) Order, 1964** by the **Ministry** **of Home Affairs**, not the Ministry of External Affairs. **So, statement III is not correct**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Tribunals in India:**
● **Article 323A** empowers Parliament to provide for the establishment of **administrative tribunals** for the adjudication of disputes relating to **recruitment and conditions of service** of persons appointed to public services of the Centre, the States, local bodies, public corporations and other public authorities.
● Parliament enacted the **Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985**, which authorised the Central Government to establish the **Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT)** and **State Administrative** **Tribunals (SATs)**.
● The **Central Administrative Tribunal** was set up in **1985** with its **Principal Bench at New Delhi** and additional benches across the country.
● CAT is a **multi-member body** consisting of a **Chairman and Members** drawn from judicial and administrative streams.
● The **State Administrative Tribunals** are established on the request of State Governments and exercise original jurisdiction over service matters of State government employees.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Lok Adalats:
I. - They do not have jurisdiction over offences
which are non-compoundable under any law.
II. - The Permanent Lok Adalat (PLA) has a pecuniary jurisdiction of up to ₹10 lakh for disputes involving Public Utility Services.
III. Unlike regular Lok Adalats, which handle
pre-litigation matters, a PLA can only take up cases already pending in a court.
IV. A party cannot make an appeal to any court
against the award made by a Lok Adalat.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The system of **Lok Adalats** is rooted in the constitutional mandate of **Article 39A**, which directs the State to provide **free legal aid** to ensure that justice is not denied to any citizen due to economic or other disabilities. This mandate is reinforced by **Articles 14 and 22(1)**, which guarantee **equality before law** and **fair legal procedure**.
● Under the **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987**, a **Lok Adalat has no jurisdiction over offences which** **are non-compoundable under any law**. Therefore, even if both parties give consent, **non-** **compoundable offences cannot be referred to a Lok Adalat**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Permanent Lok Adalat (PLA)** has a **pecuniary jurisdiction of up to ₹10 lakh**, as provided in the Act. However, the **Central Government is empowered to increase this limit**, and accordingly, in **2015,** **it was enhanced to ₹1 crore** to disputes involving Public Utility Services. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● A **Permanent Lok Adalat deals only with pre-litigation disputes** relating to **public utility services** such as transport, postal, power, water supply, hospital services and insurance. Once a matter is taken up by a PLA, **no party can approach a court for the same dispute**. On the other hand, a **regular Lok** **Adalat can take up both pending cases and pre-litigation matters**. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● An **award of a Lok Adalat is deemed to be a decree of a Civil Court** and is **final and binding on all** **the parties**. The Act explicitly states that **no appeal shall lie to any court against the award of a Lok** **Adalat**. This ensures speedy and final settlement of disputes without prolonged litigation. So, **Statement** **IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Lok Adalats:**
● Parliament enacted the **Legal Services Authorities Act, 1987**, which came into force on **9** **November 1995** and created a nationwide framework for providing free and competent legal services.
● The **National Legal Services Authority (NALSA)** was established under this Act to frame policies, monitor implementation, and ensure access to justice for weaker sections through State, District, Taluk and Court-level legal services bodies.
● There are three broad types of Lok Adalats: **National Lok Adalats**, which are held periodically across the country on specific subject matters; **State or Regular Lok Adalats**, which include continuous, daily, mobile and mega Lok Adalats; and **Permanent Lok Adalats (PLAs)**.
● **Permanent Lok Adalats**, introduced through the **2002 amendment** to the Legal Services Authorities Act, deal specifically with **disputes relating to public utility services** such as transport, postal and telecom services, power and water supply, hospitals and insurance.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023:
I. - It explicitly defines and penalises ‘mob
lynching’.
II. It provides for the conduct of a trial in absentia
for proclaimed offenders.
III. It mandates that a trial court must deliver its
judgment within 90 days of the completion of arguments.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023** replaced the **Criminal Procedure Code, 1973** **(CrPC)** and deals only with **procedural aspects** such as investigation, arrest, prosecution and bail.
● The BNSS **does not contain any explicit definition or penal provision on “mob lynching.”** Such offences, if any, are dealt with under the **substantive penal law**, not in the procedural code. BNSS only lays down **procedures** for investigation and trial. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ **The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023,** which replaced the Indian Penal Code, specifically addresses mob lynching under Section 103(2). Section 103(2) defines murder committed by a group of five or more persons on grounds such as race, caste, community, sex, place of birth, language, or personal belief as a distinct offence.
● The BNSS provides that **if a proclaimed offender has absconded to evade trial and there is no** **immediate prospect of arrest**, the **trial may be conducted and judgment pronounced in his absence**. This introduces the concept of **trial in absentia**, which did not exist under the CrPC. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● The BNSS prescribes **strict timelines for criminal procedures**. It mandates that a **trial court must** **deliver its judgment within 30 days of the completion of arguments**, although this period can be **extended up to 45 days** for recorded reasons. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS):**
● The **Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023,** is a procedural law enacted by the **Ministry of** **Home Affairs** to replace the **Criminal Procedure Code, 1973 (CrPC)**.
● It governs the procedure for **investigation, arrest, prosecution, trial, bail and custody** in criminal cases and retains the basic framework of the CrPC while introducing several new features aimed at efficiency and the use of technology.
● One of the key reforms under BNSS is the **mandatory use of forensic investigation** for offences punishable with **seven years or more of imprisonment**, requiring forensic experts to visit crime scenes and record evidence electronically.
● The law also allows **all trials, inquiries and proceedings to be conducted in electronic mode**, and permits the production of **digital devices and electronic communications** as evidence.
● The BNSS modifies **police custody and bail provisions**, allowing **15 days of police custody to be** **spread over the first 40 or 60 days of detention**, and restricting mandatory bail in cases involving **multiple charges or life imprisonment**.

questions.questionText:
With regard to the amendment to the Constitution of India, consider the following pairs:
Constitutional Amendment
Change/ Modification
I. - 24th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1971
Provided that Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution
II. - 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003
Introduced limits on the size of the Council of Ministers at the Union and State levels
III. 105th
Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021
Provided reservation for the economically weaker sections
IV. 106th
Constitutional Amendment Act, 2023
Empowered States to prepare their own lists of Socially and Economically Backward Classes
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **24th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1971,** was enacted to overturn earlier Supreme Court judgments. It explicitly provided that **Parliament has the power to amend any part of the Constitution,** **including Fundamental Rights**, and that a constitutional amendment is not a “law” under Article 13. **So,** **pair I is correctly matched**.
● The **91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003,** introduced major reforms to curb political defection. It limited the size of the **Council of Ministers to 15% of the** strength of the Lok Sabha or the State Legislative Assembly, respectively and also disqualified defectors from being appointed as ministers. **So,** **pair II is correctly matched**.
● The **105th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2021** clarified that the **Central List of Socially and** **Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs)** is to be maintained by the Union, and it also restored the power of States and Union Territories to prepare and maintain their own SEBC lists for their purposes by law. **So, pair III is not correctly matched**.
○ The **103rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019,** provided 10% reservation in public employment and higher education for economically weaker sections.
● The **106th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2023**, also known as the **Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam**, provides **33% reservation for women in the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies**. **So, pair IV** **is not correctly matched**. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the membership of the Parliament of India:
I. 	 A person elected to both the Lok Sabha and
a State Legislature must resign the State
Legislature seat within 14 days, failing which
the Lok Sabha seat becomes vacant.
II. 	 If an independent member joins any political
party after the expiry of six months, s/he is
disqualified.
III. 	As per the Representation of the People
Act, 1951, if a person is detained under a
preventive detention law, he is disqualified
from being a Member of Parliament.
How many of the statements given above are **not**
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to the **Prohibition of Simultaneous Membership Rules (1950**) made by the President **a** **person cannot be a member of both the Parliament and the state legislature** at the same time. If a person is so elected, **his seat in Parliament becomes vacant** if he does **not resign his seat in the state** **legislature within 14 days. So, statement I is correct**.
● Under the **Tenth Schedule**, an **independent member** becomes disqualified **if he or she joins any** **political party at any time after the election**. The **six-month rule applies only to nominated members**, not to independent members. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The **Representation of the People (RP) Act, 1951**, **does not disqualify** an individual from being a Member of Parliament solely for being under preventive detention; however, while **Section 62(5)** prevents detained persons from voting, it explicitly exempts preventive detention from this voting ban. **So,** **statement III is not correct**. **Therefore, option (b) is the .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Membership of Parliament:**
● If a sitting member of one House is elected to the other House, the seat in the first House is automatically vacated.
● A seat also becomes vacant if a Member of Parliament incurs any **disqualification** mentioned in the Constitution, including **defection under the Tenth Schedule**.
● The **Tenth Schedule (Anti-Defection Law)**, added by the **52nd Constitutional Amendment Act,** **1985**, provides detailed rules on disqualification for defection.
●  A **party member** is disqualified if he or she **voluntarily gives up party membership** or votes against the party’s direction without permission.
● Members may also vacate their seats through **resignation**, which must be accepted by the Speaker or Chairman, or through **absence** if they remain absent for **60 days without permission**.
● A seat becomes vacant if the election is declared void by a court, if the member is expelled by the House, or if the member is elected as **President or Vice-President**, or appointed as a **Governor**.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to Article 25 of the Constitution of India:
I. 	 It guarantees the freedom of religion to both
individuals and religious denominations.
II. 	 It covers religious practices but does not
cover religious doctrines.
III. 	It is available to both citizens and non-
citizens.
IV. 	 It is subject to restrictions of morality and
health.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Article 25** of the **Constitution of India** guarantees the **freedom of conscience and the right to profess,** **practise and propagate religion to individuals**, **Article 26** guarantees similar religious freedom to **religious denominations or their sections**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Article 25 clearly covers **both religious beliefs and religious practices**, including worship, rituals and ceremonies. **So, statement II is not correct.**
○ Doctrine is a belief (or system of beliefs) accepted as authoritative by some group or school.
● The rights under **Article 25 are available to all persons**, which includes **citizens as well as non-citizens**. The freedom of conscience and religion is not restricted only to Indian citizens. **So, statement III is** **correct.**
● The freedom guaranteed under **Article 25 is subject to public order, morality and health**, and also to other provisions relating to Fundamental Rights. This means the State can impose reasonable restrictions in these interests. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Right to Freedom of Religion:**
● The **Right to Freedom of Religion** is guaranteed under **Articles 25 to 28** of the Indian Constitution.
● **Article 25** provides that **all persons** are equally entitled to **freedom of conscience** and the right to **freely profess, practise and propagate religion**.
● The Constitution clarifies that in this context, **Hindus include Sikhs, Jains and Buddhists**, and that **wearing and carrying kirpans** is part of the profession of the Sikh religion.
● **Article 26** guarantees rights to **religious denominations or their sections**, giving them the freedom to **establish and maintain religious and charitable institutions**, manage their own religious affairs, own and acquire property, and administer such property according to law.
● The Supreme Court has laid down that a **religious denomination** must have **a common faith, a** **common organisation and a distinctive name**. On this basis, bodies like the **Ramakrishna Mission** and **Ananda Marga** have been recognised as religious denominations, while the **Aurobindo Society** has not.

questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are valid constitutional or statutory routes for the removal of a Chief Minister of a State in India?
I. - By the Governor, when the Chief Minister loses the confidence of the State Legislative Assembly
II. 	 By the President of India, upon the
proclamation of a National Emergency under Article 352
III. 	By the Speaker of the State Legislative
Assembly, through disqualification under the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution of India
IV. 	 By the Governor, if the Chief Minister fails
to become a member of the State Legislature within six months of appointment
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Under the **Parliamentary System of government in the States in India, the** **Governor of the State is** **the nominal (de jure) executive**, while the **Chief Minister is the real (de facto) executive**. The Chief Minister is the **head of the government** in the State, just as the Prime Minister is at the Centre.
● S/he  is appointed by the **Governor under Article 164 of the Constitution of India**. Where no party has a clear majority, the Governor exercises **discretion** to appoint the person most likely to command majority support and asks him or her to **prove confidence on the floor of the House**.
● A person **need not be a member of the State Legislature at the time of appointment**. Such a person can be appointed as Chief Minister for **six months**, within which he or she must get elected to the State Legislature; failing this, the person **ceases to be the Chief Minister**. **So, point IV is correct.**
● The Chief Minister is the real executive; if he or she **loses the confidence of the State Legislative** **Assembly**, the Governor can require the Chief Minister to **prove a majority**, and upon failure, the s/he is removed. **So, point I is correct.**
● During a **National Emergency (Article 352)**, the federal structure becomes quasi-unitary, but the **State** **Government and State Legislature are not dismissed or dissolved;** they continue to function, although the Centre gains concurrent power to legislate on State subjects. Removal of a Chief Minister (along with the CoM) by the President of India occurs under Article 356 (President's Rule), not Article 352. **So, point** **II is not correct.**
● While the **Speaker can disqualify a member** (including the CM) under the Tenth Schedule (antidefection law), this disqualification is for being a member of the House, **not a direct removal from the**
**post of Chief Minister.** Disqualification causes a vacancy, but the Speaker does not directly "remove" the CM from office; the seat becomes vacant upon disqualification. **So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.questionText: In the 2026 General Elections, a candidate for the Lok Sabha uses their personal social media handle to post a video where a prominent religious leader “blesses” the candidate and warns followers that voting for any other candidate would be a “spiritual sin.” The rival candidate challenges the election results in the High Court. Based on the principles of Indian democracy and established judicial precedents, how is the Court likely to interpret this scenario?

options.a:
The election is valid because the religious
leader is an independent citizen with a Fundamental Right to Freedom of Speech under Article 19(1)(a).
options.b:
The election is void because using “spiritual
pressure” to interfere with the free exercise of a voter’s choice constitutes “Undue Influence” under Section 123(2) of the Representation of the People Act (RPA), 1951.
options.c:
The election is valid provided the candidate
did not personally pay the religious leader, as “Corrupt Practice” under RPA, 1951 requires a direct financial transaction or bribery.
options.d:
The election is valid because the appeal was
made through a personal social media account and not through a formal religious institution or official campaign office.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **religious leader invoked spiritual fear** by declaring that voting against a particular candidate would be a **“spiritual sin.”** This directly interferes with the **free exercise of a voter’s choice**, which is the core of **electoral democracy**.
● Under **Section 123(2) of the Representation of the People Act, 1951**, **“undue influence”** includes **any** **direct or indirect interference with the free exercise of an electoral right**, including the use of **religious** **or spiritual threats, inducements or moral pressure**.
● By posting and circulating the video on one’s **own social media handle**, the candidate has **adopted and** **amplified** the message, making it part of his election campaign.
● The Supreme Court has held that **free and fair elections require voters to be free from hidden** **influences, coercion and manipulation**. The Court has emphasised that **electoral choice must be** **informed and voluntary**, not shaped by **fear, pressure or moral intimidation**. A message that equates voting choice with spiritual sin directly violates this democratic freedom. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**10.**
**Answer: c**
**Explanation:**
● When the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)** purchases government securities from the public through **Open** **Market Operations (OMOs)**, it injects liquidity into the economy. The immediate and definite monetary impact occurs through the creation of **reserve money (M0)** and an expansion in **broad money (M3).**
● **First, M0 (Reserve Money)** increases immediately. RBI pays for the securities by crediting the **accounts** **of banks (or intermediaries)** with the RBI. This raises bankers’ **deposits with the RBI, a core** **component of M0** (which consists of currency in circulation plus bankers’ deposits with RBI). **Hence,** **M0 must rise at once.**
● **Second, M3 (Broad Money)** also increases immediately. When the RBI buys securities from the public, the sellers receive **payment typically as bank deposits. Bank deposits are a major component of M3** (which includes currency with the public, demand deposits, time deposits, and other deposits). Therefore, the moment the transaction is settled, **deposits in the banking system rise, expanding M3.**
● While **M1 (currency with public + demand deposits) may increase**, it is **not guaranteed** in every case because the payment could be credited to time deposits. Hence, **M1 is not “definitely” affected.** Measures like **M2 or M4 are not standard policy targets** in India and need not change immediately. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The RBI has announced OMO purchases of government securities worth ₹1 trillion and a three-year dollar–rupee buy/sell swap of $5 billion to inject liquidity into the financial system.

questions.questionText:
With regard to the Index of Eight Core Industries,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The current base year for calculating the
index is 2022-23.
II. 	 Among the eight core industries, crude oil
has the highest weight, while cement has the lowest weight.
III. 	The overall year-on-year growth rate of the
Index has remained positive in the last ten years except during 2020–21.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Index of Eight Core Industries (ICI)** measures the production performance of **eight key sectors** that drive India’s industrial growth, including **Coal, Steel, Electricity, and Refinery Products.** These core industries are significant as they contribute **40.27% to the IIP** and influence overall economic activity and other industries.
● The Index is compiled and released by the **Office of the Economic Adviser** under the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), **Ministry of Commerce and Industry.** It is issued **monthly** through a Press Information Bureau release.
● The **base year** of the Index of Eight Core Industries is **2011–12. = 100.** This base year was **revised from** **the earlier 2004-05 s**eries to align with the IIP and Gross Domestic Product (GDP) series, ensuring structural changes in the economy are captured accurately. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Petroleum Refinery Products** carry the **highest weight** in the ICI at 28.04%, reflecting their importance in industrial activity. **Fertilizers** have the **lowest weight** at 2.63%, as per the IIP-derived weighting structure. **So, statement II is not correct.**
○ Sector-wise weightage (in %): Petroleum Refinery Products (28.04%), Electricity (19.85%), Steel (17.92%), Coal (10.33%), Crude Oil (8.98%), Natural Gas (6.88%), Cement (5.37%), and Fertilizers (2.63%).
● The ICI recorded **positive growth rates** consistently from **2014-15 through 2024-25**, ranging from 0.4% to 10.4%. The **only exception** was the **pandemic**-impacted financial year **2020-21,** where the growth rate contracted to (-) 6.4%. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The combined Index of Eight Core Industries (ICI) recorded a 4.3% growth in November 2024 compared to November 2023.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Monetary Policy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following scenarios:
I. 	 Excess aggregate demand in the economy
II. 	 Credit expansion leading to higher consumption
III. 	Increase in fuel and food prices due to supply
shocks
IV. 	 Disruptions in global supply chains
V. 	 Structural deficiencies in agricultural marketing and logistics In how many of the cases given above is monetary policy likely to be effective in directly controlling inflation?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Monetary policy** refers to actions taken by the **central bank**, such as changing **interest** rates, **liquidity** conditions, or **credit** availability, **to control inflation** and stabilize the economy. It is most effective against **demand-side inflation**, but limited in addressing **supply-side shocks.**
● When **aggregate demand** exceeds the economy’s productive capacity, **inflationary pressures** arise. **Monetary policy** can **effectively** curb such demand-pull inflation by **raising interest rates, reducing** **liquidity,** and dampening consumption and investment. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Easy credit conditions** encourage borrowing and spending, fueling **inflation**. Central banks can tighten monetary policy by **increasing policy rates**, restricting credit growth, and raising reserve requirements, thereby **reducing consumption demand.** Thus, monetary policy is effective in controlling inflation arising from credit expansion. **So, point II is correct.**
● Inflation caused by **supply shocks**, such as poor harvests or spikes in global oil prices, is **cost-push in** **nature.** Monetary policy **cannot directly** **increase** supply or **reduce** global prices. **Tightening policy** may **even hurt** growth without lowering prices, making it largely ineffective here. **So, point III is not** **correct.**
● **Global supply chain disruptions** reduce the availability of goods, raising prices. Monetary policy has limited influence over **international logistics**, trade barriers, or geopolitical disruptions. While it can
manage secondary demand effects, it **cannot resolve the root cause of such inflation**, so it is ineffective. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Structural deficiencies** in agricultural marketing like lack of cold storage, poor transport **infrastructure**, or **cartelization (hoarding)** cause artificial scarcity and inflation. These are structural/administrative issues that require **long-term infrastructure investment** and administrative **reforms**. **Monetary** **tightening cannot fix** market inefficiencies.  **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The Reserve Bank of India’s Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) has cut the repo rate by 25 basis points, reducing it to 5.25%. This marks a cumulative cut of 125 bps in 2025.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With regard to the presence of foreign banks in
India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 India follows the principles of reciprocity
and single mode of presence with respect to foreign banks.
II. 	 As per India’s WTO commitments, foreign
banks may open up to 12 branches annually.
III. 	Automatic expansion is restricted if foreign
banks’ capital and reserves in India exceed 20% of the total Indian banking system.
Which of the statements given is/are above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **India allows foreign banks** to operate either through **branches** or as **Wholly Owned Subsidiaries** **(WOS)** under a framework notified by the **Reserve Bank of India.** The policy aims to **balance financial** **stability,** market access, and reciprocity while aligning with India’s international commitments.
○ The banking subsidiary will be governed by the provisions of **the Companies Act, 1956, Banking** **Regulation Act, 1949, Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934**, other relevant statutes and the directives, prudential regulations and other guidelines/instructions issued by RBI and other regulators from time to time.
● The RBI’s framework for foreign banks is guided by **two cardinal principles:** **reciprocity**, meaning Indian banks should receive similar treatment abroad, and a **single mode of presence,** which requires a foreign bank to operate either as branches or as a WOS, not both simultaneously. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● Under India’s commitments at **the World Trade Organization**, foreign banks are allowed to open up to **12 branches per year.** RBI may permit additional branches if banks expand operations in unbanked or under-banked areas, consistent with financial inclusion objectives. **So, statement II is correct.**
● From a **financial stability** perspective, downside risk may arise if the foreign banks, i.e., **WOSs of the** **foreign banks** and foreign bank **branches** together, come to dominate the domestic financial system. To address this risk, **restrictions** would be placed on **further entry** of new WOSs of foreign banks, when the **capital and reserves** of the foreign banks (i.e., WOSs and foreign bank branches) in India **exceed** **20% of the capital and reserves** of the banking system. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Japanese mega banks have been increasingly investing in India’s banking and financial sector as they look beyond their saturated domestic market for higher-growth opportunities.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Exchange Rates (NEER, REER, Currency Mechanisms)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the inflation in the economy,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 There is an inverse relationship between
inflation rates and the market price of existing fixed-rate bonds.
II. 	 High inflation generally reduces the real value
of the government’s outstanding liabilities.
III. 	Under a floating exchange rate system,
high domestic inflation typically leads to appreciation of the domestic currency.
How many of the statements give above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Inflation** refers to a **sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services** in an economy, resulting in a **decline in the purchasing power of money**. Its effects vary across **financial markets**, **government finances**, external trade, and overall economic efficiency.
● When **inflation rises,** **interest rates** usually **increase** to compensate lenders for the loss of purchasing power. Existing fixed-rate bonds with **lower coupon rates** become **less attractive**, causing their market prices to fall. Hence, bond prices and inflation exhibit an inverse relationship. **So, statement I is correct.**
● High **inflation erodes the real (inflation-adjusted)** value of fixed-rate government debt. Since repayments are made in nominal terms, **governments effectively repay debt with “cheaper” money**, reducing the real burden of past borrowing and easing debt servicing. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **High domestic inflation** under a floating exchange rate system generally leads to **currency depreciation**, as rising prices reduce purchasing power, increase demand for foreign goods, and raise demand for foreign currency in exchange markets.**So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Retail inflation in November 2025 was recorded at 0.7%, marking the second-lowest level in the current data series, following the lowest rate in October 2025.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** If the Indian Rupee depreciates against the US Dollar, India’s export competitiveness will always increase.
**Statement II:** The Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER) adjusts the nominal exchange rate for inflation differentials between India and its major trading partners.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **depreciation of the Indian Rupee** d**oes not always increase export competitiveness.** If domestic inflation is high, **production costs rise** and may offset the price advantage gained from **currency** **depreciation.** Additionally, **weak global demand,** supply-side constraints, and dependence on **imported** **inputs** can limit export gains. Hence, depreciation alone **does not guarantee higher competitiveness.** **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER)** is a measure of a country’s currency value against a basket of currencies of major trading partners, **adjusted for relative inflation.** By accounting for price level differences, REER **reflects the true competitiveness of exports** and imports better than the nominal exchange rate. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The rupee breached ₹90 per dollar amid FPI outflows, trade deficit pressures, gold imports, and RBI’s soft-touch intervention.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Monetary Policy
questions.questionText:
A decrease in the Weighted Average Call Rate
(WACR) indicates:

options.a:
Decrease in long-term interest rates in the
economy
options.b: A decrease in the borrowing cost for the banks
options.c:
An increase in liquidity deficit in the banking
system
options.d: An increase in the borrowing cost for banks

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Weighted Average Call Rate (WACR)** is the **average interest rate at which banks borrow and** **lend overnight funds** in the **call money market** and serves as the operational target of the **Reserve Bank** **of India (RBI)** monetary policy framework. It is expected to move closely around the policy repo rate within the **Liquidity Adjustment Facility (LAF) corridor.**
● A decline in WACR indicates that **overnight funds are available at lower rates**, reflecting easier liquidity conditions in the banking system and hence a **reduction in banks’ short-term borrowing costs.**
● This relationship was evident during **September and October 2025,** as highlighted in the RBI’s monthly report **“State of the Economy”.** During this period, the WACR showed better alignment with the policy repo rate compared to the previous month:
○ In the **latter half of September,** WACR traded **above the repo rate due** to **temporary liquidity** **tightness caused by tax outflows.** ○ As liquidity conditions improved in **early October**, the **WACR eased below the policy repo rate,** **reflecting lower borrowing costs for banks.**
● Thus, movements in the WACR provide a reliable indication of liquidity conditions and the effective transmission of monetary policy and **a decrease in WACR clearly signifies a decline in banks’** **borrowing costs.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), improved liquidity conditions led to a closer alignment of the Weighted Average Call Rate (WACR) with the policy repo rate.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Foreign Direct Investment
(FDI) policy in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Proposal for FDI under the Government route
is examined and approved by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT).
II. 	 FDI proposals involving an amount exceeding
₹500 crore require approval of the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA).
III. 	Over the past five years, India has consistently
remained among the top three recipients of global FDI inflows.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)** is a key component of India’s external sector policy and an important source of non-debt capital inflows. India’s FDI regime is governed by the consolidated FDI Policy issued by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) and implemented through the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA).
● After the abolition of the Foreign Investment Promotion Board, the **authority to approve Foreign Direct** **Investment** proposals under the government route was **decentralised to the concerned Ministries and** **Departments**, while the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade acts as the nodal agency through the Foreign Investment Facilitation Portal and handles policy matters. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● The Consolidated FDI Policy Circular, 2020, states that proposals involving total foreign **equity inflow** **of more than ₹5,000 crore should be placed before the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs** **(CCEA)** by the concerned Competent Authority for consideration. This is a specific procedural provision in the policy. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **India ranked 15th globally in FDI inflows in 2024, up from 16th in 2023.** This ranking shows that India was not in the top three globally in 2024 (top three were USA, Singapore, and Hong Kong/China). India’s global rank in FDI flows has fluctuated and was outside the top three in 2023 and 2024. **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the .**
**Knowledge Box**
● The services sector emerged as the top recipient of FDI equity in FY 2024–25, attracting 19% of total inflows, followed by computer software and hardware (16%) and trading (8%).
● Maharashtra accounted for the highest share (39%) of total FDI equity inflows in FY 2024–25, followed by Karnataka (13%) and Delhi (12%).
● Among source countries, Singapore led with 30% share, followed by Mauritius (17%) and the United States (11%).
● The number of source countries for FDI increased from 89 in FY 2013–14 to 112 in FY 2024–25.
**Relevance:** FDI policy has been in the news due to record-level inflows, sector-specific liberalisation, scrutiny of investments from neighbouring countries, and government efforts to improve ease of doing business in India.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the rapeseed-mustard in India:
I. 	 It is a notified oilseed for which the
Government announces Minimum Support Price (MSP).
II. 	 It is predominantly cultivated as a Rabi
oilseed crop in India.
III. 	Its oil is primarily used for industrial purposes.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Oilseeds** are an important component of India’s agricultural economy, both for farmers’ income and for edible oil security. India is a major global producer of oilseeds, cultivating nine main types: Groundnut, Soybean, Rapeseed-Mustard, Sesame, Sunflower, Safflower, Niger, Castor, and Linseed, with Soybean, Groundnut, and Rapeseed-Mustard contributing most to production.
● **Rapeseed–mustard is one of the notified oilseeds for which the Government of India announces** **MSP** every agricultural season. The MSP is recommended by the Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP) and approved by the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA). **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● In India, **rapeseed–mustard is predominantly grown during the Rabi season**, sown in October– November and harvested in February–March. Major producing states include Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Haryana, and West Bengal. Of the total area and production under the nine oilseed crops grown in India, rapeseed-mustard accounts for 30.3% of the acreage and 33.2% of the production (202324). **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Rapeseed–mustard oil is one of the most commonly used edible oils in India**, particularly in northern, eastern, and northeastern regions. It is used for **cooking, frying, pickling, and food processing** due to its pungent flavour and suitability for Indian cuisines. So, statement III is correct. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Rapeseed–mustard has been in the news due to MSP hikes for Rabi oilseeds and the government’s efforts to reduce edible oil imports under the National Mission on Edible Oils.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Soils of India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Brown Forest Soil:
I. 	 In India, it is predominantly found in the
Deccan Plateau region.
II. 	 It is rich in organic matter and humus.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, **brown forest soils**, also known as **mountain soils**, are fertile, dark-coloured soils found **in the** **Himalayan region,** **Western Ghats, and Eastern Ghats** at higher elevation. The Deccan Plateau is predominantly characterised by black soils (regur soils), which are formed from the weathering of volcanic rocks. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Brown forest soils are generally found under **forest cover and are rich in organic matter and humus,** formed through the decomposition of forest litter. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Solar Radiation, Heat Balance, Temperature
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Between 40° N and 40° S latitudes, incoming insolation exceeds outgoing terrestrial radiation.
**Statement II:** The atmosphere is primarily heated by long-wave radiation emitted by the Earth’s surface.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Earth** maintains a **constant average temperature** because the total amount of heat received from the sun in the form of **insolation is balanced** by the total amount of heat lost by the Earth through terrestrial radiation. This equilibrium is known as the **Earth's Heat Budget.** Without this **precise balance,** the planet would become progressively hotter or colder over time, making it uninhabitable.
● **The Sun emits** energy mainly in the form of **short-wave radiation**, such as visible light and **ultraviolet** **rays.** The **Earth’s surface** absorbs a significant portion of this **incoming short-wave radiation**, which warms land and oceans. This absorbed energy later drives weather systems, ocean currents, and the **overall** **climate system**.
● The **atmosphere** absorbs only a small fraction of incoming solar radiation directly. Instead, it is **primarily** **heated from below by long-wave (infrared) radiation** emitted by the Earth’s surface. **Greenhouse** **gases** like carbon dioxide, water vapour, and methane trap this terrestrial radiation, warming the atmosphere indirectly, which is the dominant heating mechanism. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Between 40° N and 40° S**, the **Sun’s rays are relatively direct** throughout the year, resulting in high insolation. In this zone, **incoming solar radiation exceeds** outgoing terrestrial radiation, creating a net energy surplus. **This excess energy is redistributed** poleward by atmospheric circulation and ocean currents. **So, statement I is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does** **not** **explain Statement I** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Fault
Associated Seismic Region
l. Thrust Main Himalayan System
II. - Strike-Slip San Andreas Fault
III. - Normal Bhuj Fault in Kachchh
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **thrust fault** is a low-angle reverse fault formed under **compressional stress**, where **older rock layers** are pushed over younger ones, commonly seen at **convergent plate boundaries**. Classic examples include the **Main Himalayan Thrust** and the **Lewis Thrust** in North America. The Main Himalayan System is dominated by **large-scale thrusting** due to India–Eurasia collision. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● A **strike-slip fault** is characterized by **horizontal movement** of rock blocks past each other due to **shear** **stress**, with minimal vertical displacement. Well-known examples include the **San Andreas Fault (USA)** and the **North Anatolian Fault (Turkey).** The San Andreas Fault is a textbook strike-slip fault. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
● A **normal fault** forms due to **tensional forces**, where the hanging wall moves downward relative to the footwall, **typical of rift zones**. Famous examples include the **East African Rift** and the **Basin** and **Range** Province. The **Bhuj Fault in Kachchh** is an **intraplate** compressional/reverse fault system. **So, pair III** **is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Which of the following is/are directly responsible
for the initiation of the Thermohaline Circulation in oceans?
I. 	 High sea-surface temperatures in tropical
oceans
II. 	 Sea ice formation in polar regions
III. 	Strong equatorial upwelling driven by trade
winds
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Thermohaline Circulation (THC)** is a global system of deep-ocean currents driven by differences in **temperature (thermo) and salinity (haline).** Cold, salty water becomes dense and sinks at high latitudes, driving **large-scale ocean circulation** that redistributes heat, regulates climate, and influences marine ecosystems.
● **High temperatures** in **tropical oceans reduce the density of seawater, thereby preventing sinking in** **these regions.** **So, point I is not correct.**
○ Although **strong evaporation** in the tropics **increases salinity** and tends **to raise water density,** the effect of high temperature dominates locally, keeping the water buoyant.
○ This saline tropical water is later transported poleward through ocean currents to the North Atlantic, where cooling by cold winds increases its density sufficiently to cause sinking. ○ Thus, tropical processes act only as an **indirect contributor** by supplying saline water, while the direct initiation of sinking occurs in cold, high-latitude regions.
● When sea **ice forms in polar regions**, salt is expelled into the surrounding seawater, increasing **salinity** **and density.** This dense water sinks, making this a key driver of thermohaline circulation. **So, point II is** **correct.**
● **Equatorial upwelling** brings deep water to the surface but **does not involve** the sinking of surface waters. Hence, it **does not initiate** thermohaline circulation. **So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following countries:
I. 	 Algeria
II. 	 Mali
III. 	Burkina Faso
IV. 	 Ghana
How many of the above are crossed by both the Tropic of Cancer and the Prime Meridian?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Tropic of Cancer (23½° N)** passes through **16 countries** across North America, Africa, and Asia, including **Mexico, the Bahamas, Western Sahara, Mauritania, Mali, Algeria, Niger, Libya, Egypt,** **Saudi Arabia, UAE, Oman, India, Bangladesh, Myanmar, China,** and **Taiwan.**
● The **Prime Meridian (0° longitude)** passes through the **United Kingdom**, **France**, **Spain** in Europe, and **Algeria**, **Mali**, **Burkina Faso**, **Togo**, and **Ghana** in Africa, starting from the North Pole and extending to the South Pole, also crossing Antarctica. It's defined by the **Royal Observatory in Greenwich, London**, connecting these diverse regions across three continents.
● The **Tropic of Cancer** and the **Prime Meridian** pass simultaneously through **only two countries on** **Earth: Algeria and Mali. So, options I and II are correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Map-Based Relevance (Trade Routes, Ports, Countries, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Place/Site Associated River
I. - Mohenjo-daro Ravi
II. - Sanchi Stupa Betwa
III. - Amaravati Stupa Godavari
IV. - Ajanta Caves Moosi
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Many important archaeological and historical sites in the Indian subcontinent developed in close association with river systems. Rivers provided water, fertile alluvial plains, trade routes, and cultural continuity, making them central to the growth of ancient urban centres, religious establishments, and rockcut architecture.
● **Mohenjo-daro,** one of the principal cities of the Harappan (Indus Valley) Civilization, was **located on** **the right bank of the Indus River** (in present-day Sindh, Pakistan). While some Harappan sites such as Harappa are associated with the Ravi River, Mohenjo-daro itself is not. **So, pair I is not correctly** **matched.**
● **The Sanchi Stupa complex** is situated in present-day Madhya Pradesh, near Vidisha. It **lies close to the** **Betwa River**, a tributary of the Yamuna. **Ancient Vidisha**, an important **urban centre during the** **Mauryan and post-Mauryan periods**, also flourished due to its proximity to the Betwa. Hence, the pairing of Sanchi Stupa with the Betwa River is correct. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **The Amaravati Stupa**, an important centre of early Buddhist art and architecture, is **located on the banks** **of the Krishna River in Andhra Pradesh**. The prosperity of the Krishna river valley played a key role in the development of Buddhism and trade in the Deccan during the Satavahana period. **So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.**
● The **Ajanta Caves** are situated in Maharashtra along the **Waghora River**, a tributary of the Godavari. The **Moosi River,** on the other hand, flows near **Hyderabad** and has no geographical or historical association with the Ajanta caves.  **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Archaeological sites such as Sanchi, Amaravati, and Ajanta have recently been in the news due to conservation efforts by the Archaeological Survey of India, UNESCO-related heritage discussions, and concerns over sustainable tourism and river-linked heritage landscapes.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water on Earth Surface, Ocean Relief
questions.questionText:
The Northern Indian Ocean lacks a permanent
circular gyre system as found in the Atlantic and the Pacific Oceans. Which one of the following best explains the reason behind this?

options.a:
The Coriolis force is too weak in the Indian
Ocean region
options.b:
There is no anti-cyclonic circulation of ocean
currents in the Indian Ocean
options.c:
The Indian Ocean remains under the
permanent influence of the Westerlies throughout the year
options.d:
The Indian Ocean has monsoon drift that
causes a seasonal reversal in the wind direction

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● An ocean **gyre is a large system of circulating ocean currents**. Wind patterns and the rotation of Earth cause spiral or circular currents to form in the ocean. These currents are called gyres.
● Three forces cause the circular motion of a gyre: **global wind patterns, Earth’s rotation, and Earth’s** **landmasses.**
○ Wind drags on the ocean surface water, causing water to move in the direction of the wind. ○ Earth’s rotation causes the **Coriolis effect**, which deflects the ocean currents and changes their direction. In the Northern Hemisphere, ocean currents are deflected to the right, in a **clockwise** **motion.** In the Southern Hemisphere, ocean currents are pushed to the left, in a **counterclockwise** **motion.** ○ Earth’s continents and other landmasses (such as islands) also influence the creation of ocean gyres.
● There are five major gyres: **the North Pacific Subtropical Gyre, the South Pacific Subtropical Gyre,** **the North Atlantic Subtropical Gyre, the South Atlantic Subtropical Gyre, and the Indian Ocean** **Subtropical Gyre.**
● Most ocean gyres are very stable and predictable. For example, the North Atlantic Ocean Gyre always flows in a steady, clockwise path around the North Atlantic Ocean.
● However, some gyres experience seasonal variation, like the Indian Ocean Gyre. ○ The Indian Ocean Gyre is a complex system of many currents extending from the eastern coast of Africa to the western coast of Australia. The northern part of the system circulates between the Horn of Africa and the Indonesian archipelago. ○ The northern part of the Indian Ocean Gyre is **not permanent and seasonally changes direction**. This region lacks a permanent gyre system **because it is affected by monsoonal winds,** and thus the current direction changes seasonally with the wind patterns. **In the summer, the current flows** **clockwise, as the monsoon blows in from the southwestern Indian Ocean.** **In the winter, the** **current flows counterclockwise,** as the wind blows in from the Tibetan plateau in the northeast. ○ The northern Indian Ocean Gyre is a much smaller ocean gyre as compared to the South Pacific Gyre. It is because of the presence of landmasses. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Aquatic Ecosystem and Life (Aquatic Ecosystem, Maritime Zones, Corals, Mangrove, Marine Vegetation)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following best describes the concept
of permaculture?

options.a:
Maximizing crop yield through the intensive
use of chemical fertilizers
options.b:
Designing human settlements and agricultural
systems that mimic the patterns and relationships found in nature
options.c:
Promoting large-scale monoculture to ensure
global food security
options.d:
Developing genetically modified seeds that
are resistant to all pests

questions.explanationMarkdown: ● **Permaculture** is an ethical design approach rooted in ecological principles that works with nature rather than controlling it. By **mimicking natural ecosystem patterns** such as biodiversity, layered vegetation, and closed nutrient cycles, it aims to create sustainable, self-sufficient agricultural and human systems that recycle resources and minimize waste. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is **not**
correct with regard to the Government of India Act, 1919?

options.a:
It introduced a limited franchise, under which
certain women were granted the right to vote for the first time.
options.b:
It introduced diarchy in the provinces, dividing
subjects into reserved and transferred categories.
options.c:
It empowered the Governor to resume the
administration of transferred subjects in the event of a breakdown of the constitutional machinery within the province.
options.d:
It introduced bicameralism in the provincial
legislatures.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Government of India Act, 1919**, enacted on the basis of the **Montagu–Chelmsford Report**, marked a significant but cautious step towards responsible government in British India. The Act introduced a **limited and restricted franchise** based on property, tax payment, and education. Within this narrow framework, **certain women were granted the right to vote for the first time**, subject to provincial regulations and qualifications. Although the electorate remained very small, this marked the first formal political recognition of women in Indian electoral politics.
● A key feature of the Act was the **introduction of diarchy in provincial governments**, dividing subjects into **‘reserved’ subjects** (such as law and order, finance, and land revenue) controlled by the Governor and his executive council, and **‘transferred’ subjects** (such as education, public health, and agriculture) administered by Indian ministers responsible to the provincial legislative council.
● The Act also empowered the **Governor to take over the administration of transferred subjects** if there was a **failure of constitutional machinery** in the province. This ensured that ultimate authority remained with the British executive, limiting real autonomy for Indian ministers.
● While the Act introduced **bicameralism at the central level**, creating the **Council of State** and the **Central Legislative Assembly**, **provincial legislatures remained unicameral**, though their membership was expanded and partly elected. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Buddhism, Jainism & Other Philosophies
questions.questionText:
The Piprahwa Relics, discovered in 1898 by
British engineer William C. Peppe, were found at the Piprahwa Stupa, which is located at the site widely identified as?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Piprahwa Relics,** discovered in 1898 by William C. Peppé at the Piprahwa Stupa, are **widely** **associated with Kapilavastu**, the ancient capital of the **Shakya republic.** Kapilavastu is of central importance in Buddhism as the place where Siddhartha Gautama spent his childhood and early youth before renouncing worldly life.
● **Kushinagar** is one of the most sacred Buddhist sites as **it is the place where the Buddha attained** **Mahaparinirvana.** Along with Lumbini, Bodh Gaya, and Sarnath, it **forms one of the four principal** **pilgrimage centres of Buddhism.** While Kushinagar is associated with relic worship and stupas, it is not connected with the Piprahwa relic discovery, which occurred at a different site.
● **Vaishali** was the capital of the Licchavi republic and holds major importance in Buddhist history. **It was** **the venue of the Second Buddhist Council, the place where the Buddha delivered his last sermon,** **and where women were first admitted into the Sangha.** Despite its prominence in early Buddhism, Vaishali has no historical or archaeological link to the Piprahwa relics.
● **Avanti** was one of the sixteen Mahajanapadas, with its capital at Ujjain, and played a significant role in the early spread of Buddhism in western India. **It was an important monastic and intellectual centre,** **associated with early Buddhist schools.** However, Avanti is a regional–political entity rather than a relic site, and it has no association with the Piprahwa Stupa or relics. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Sacred Piprahwa Relics of Lord Buddha Return Home to India after 127 years.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Treaty Signatories Main Provision / Significance
I. - Treaty of Tordesillas
Spain and Portugal
Divided the newly discovered world between Spain and Portugal
II. - Treaty of Alinagar
British East India Company and Nawab of Bengal
Restored British trading privileges
III. - Treaty of Allahabad
British East India Company and Mughal Emperor
Granted Diwani rights of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa to the British
IV. - Treaty of Sugauli
British East India Company and Nepal
Defined Nepal’s boundaries and allowed a British Resident in Kathmandu
V. - Treaty of Pondicherry
British East India Company and France
Brought an end to the Second Carnatic War
In which of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?

options.c: II, III, IV and V only
options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Treaties played a decisive role in shaping European expansion and British political dominance in India. They defined spheres of influence, trade privileges, territorial control and administrative authority. From early European rivalries to British imperial consolidation, treaties were key instruments of power.
● **The Treaty of Tordesillas (1494)** was signed **between Spain and Portugal** to **divide newly discovered** **lands outside Europe.** It indirectly influenced access to maritime routes to Asia and India by allocating spheres of colonial expansion. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● **The Treaty of Alinagar (1757),** signed **between Siraj-ud-Daulah, Nawab of Bengal and the East India** **Company after the Battle of Plassey**, restored British trading privileges, allowed **fortification of** **Calcutta** and **granted the Company the right to mint coins.** **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● **The Treaty of Allahabad (1765),** concluded **between the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II and the** **East India Company, granted the Diwani rights** of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa to the Company, marking the foundation of British political power in India. **So, row III is correctly matched.**
● **The Treaty of Sugauli (1816),** signed **between the British East India Company and Nepal after the** **Anglo-Nepalese War**, fixed Nepal’s boundaries and allowed the appointment of a British Resident at Kathmandu. **So, row IV is correctly matched.**
● The **Treaty of Pondicherry** was concluded between the **British East India Company and the French** **in 1754** to bring an end to the **Second Carnatic War** in India. Under this treaty, both European powers agreed to **restore each other’s conquered territories** and maintain the existing balance of power in the Carnatic region. Importantly, it led to the **recall of Dupleix**, the French Governor-General, thereby weakening French political ambitions in India. Although the treaty temporarily ended open hostilities, it failed to resolve underlying rivalries, and both powers soon resumed interference in Indian affairs, ultimately leading to the **Third Carnatic War**.  **So, row V is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Finance Commission
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
British Commission Subject
I. - Strachey Commission
Famine measure
II. - Raleigh Commission University Education
III. Whitley Commission Labour conditions
IV. Hilton Young
Commission
Relations with Princely States
V. - Chatfield Commission
Agriculture
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Great Famine of 1876–1878,** which occurred during the tenure of **Lord Lytton,** was intensified by severe drought conditions and his policy of allowing the export of grain to Britain. In response, the **Famine** **Commission** was appointed in **1880 under the chairmanship of Richard Strachey** to examine the **causes of famines and suggest measures to prevent their recurrence.** The Commission recommended several corrective steps, including the expansion of irrigation facilities, the introduction of relief works during periods of drought and the reduction of land revenue and taxes in famine-affected regions. **So, pair** **I is correctly matched.**
● The **Raleigh Commission** was constituted in **1902** to examine the **conditions and prospects of** **universities in India** and to recommend measures for improving their structure and functioning. Its mandate was limited to university education and it was expressly barred from reporting on primary or secondary education. Based on the Commission’s recommendations, the **Indian Universities Act was** **enacted in 1904. So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Whitley Commission**, officially the **Royal Commission on Labour (1931–33)**, **examined wage** **determination in India**, including wage levels, minimum wages, standardisation and incentives. It stressed systematic collection of wage data and recommended establishing machinery for fixing minimum wages, while also highlighting unfair wage deductions by employers. These **recommendations** **contributed to the enactment of the Payment of Wages Act, 1936.** **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● The **Hilton Young Commission,** formally known as the **Royal Commission on Indian Currency and** **Finance,** was appointed in **1926** by the British government. It played a pivotal role by recommending the
establishment of a central bank for India. These recommendations directly led to the creation of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) in 1935, entrusted with the regulation of currency and the management of credit in the country. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
● The **Chatfield Commission (1939)**, constituted during the Viceroyship of Lord Linlithgow, examined issues relating to Indian defence, particularly India’s role and financial responsibility in the imperial defence system on the eve of the Second World War. The Commission aimed to recommend measures for modernising the Indian Army and ensuring its effective integration into the imperial defence system. **So,** **pair V is not correctly matched.** **So, only three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Buddhism, Jainism & Other Philosophies
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following is **not** one of the
‘Three Universal Truths’ in Buddhist philosophy?

options.a: The world is transient and constantly changing
options.b:
Relativity and multiplicity of truth, where
multiple viewpoints can all be partially correct
options.c:
The soul is non-eternal and non-permanent in
nature
options.d: Sorrow is intrinsic to human existence

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In Buddhism, the **Three Marks of Existence (Tilakkhaṇa / Triratna)** are three universal characteristics of existence which are central to the Buddhist Philosophy. They are:
○ **Anicca** which states that the world is transient  and constantly changing. ○ Nothing lasts forever and all physical and mental phenomena arise, exist, and pass away. ○ **Anatta,** that is the existence is soulless, as there is nothing permanent or eternal in it. There is no permanent, unchanging self or soul in living beings. What we call the “self” is just a collection of changing physical and mental components. ○ **Dukkha** (Suffering or Unsatisfactoriness) explains that life is inherently unsatisfactory and subject to suffering because nothing permanent can bring lasting happiness. This includes physical suffering, mental distress, and the general sense of incompleteness.
● **Anekantavada** on the other hand **is a** **fundamental doctrine of Jain philosophy** that emphasizes the **relativity and multiplicity of truth**. Literally meaning “no-one‑perspective‑ism,” it asserts that **reality is complex and multifaceted**, and no single viewpoint can capture the whole truth by itself. According to this doctrine, different perspectives may each reflect **partial aspects of a deeper truth**, much like the classic parable of blind men touching different parts of an elephant and describing it differently, yet each description contains an element of truth. **Therefore, option (b) is the .**
**Knowledge Box**
● The Buddha regarded the **social world as the creation of humans rather than of divine origin.** Therefore, he advised kings and gahapatis to be humane and ethical. Individual effort was expected to transform social relations.
● The Buddha emphasised individual agency and **righteous action as the means to escape from** **the cycle of rebirth and attain self-realisation and nibbana**, literally the extinguishing of the ego and desire, and thus end the cycle of suffering for those who renounced the world. According to Buddhist tradition, his last words to his followers were: **“Be lamps unto yourselves as all of** **you must work out your own liberation.”**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following types of pottery in India in
chronological order of their origin:
I. 	 Painted Grey Ware
II. 	 Northern Black Polished Ware
III. 	Rouletted ware
IV. 	 Glazed ware
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Pottery is often called the **"Lyric of handicrafts"** for its poetic form. It is one of the earliest crafts practiced by humans. The earliest evidence of pottery comes from the **Neolithic site of Mehrgarh** (in present-day Pakistan), showing a highly developed clay pottery tradition as early as **6000 BC**.
● The dates of the **Painted Grey Ware (PGW)** culture range from c. 1100 to c. 500/400 BCE, and the sites in the north-west are probably earlier than those in the Ganga valley.
● The PGW phase is followed by the **Northern Black Polished Ware (NBPW)** phase, the beginning of
which goes back to c. 700 BCE at Sringaverapura. The evidence from various PGW sites suggests a protourban phase.
● Pottery of the **Rouletted ware** family belongs to India’s Early Historic period (c. 500 BC to c. AD 200), and it was found in South India, especially at **Arikamedu** near Puducherry.
● The era of **glazed ware began in the 12th century AD.** This type of pottery contains a white background and has blue and green patterns. The glazed Harappan pottery is the earliest example of its kind in the ancient world. In India, the making of Glazed pottery came into being with the advent of the Arab influence in India.
○ Glazed pottery with a white background and blue and green patterns is developed in Delhi, Amritsar, Jaipur, Khurja, Chunar and Rampur in Uttar Pradesh, and Karigari in Tamil Nadu. **So, the correct chronological order of origin of the types of pottery in India is: Painted Grey Ware -** **Northern Black Polished Ware - Rouletted ware - Glazed ware.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Points to Remember**
**Red and Black Pottery:** In some parts of the country, evidence of red and black pottery can be found that has been dated to 1500-300 BC. These were found in large parts of West Bengal.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to “antiquity”, under the
Antiquities and Art Treasures Act, 1972, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Any manuscript to be considered an antiquity
should be in existence for not less than seventy five years.
II. 	 An article must have been in existence for not
less than one hundred years to be declared an antiquity.
III. 	The Act explicitly declares sacred temple
sculptures to be living entities.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Antiquities and Art Treasures Act, 1972** is a legislation enacted to **regulate the export of** **antiquities and art treasures.** It seeks to prevent the smuggling of and fraudulent dealings in such objects
and provides for their **compulsory acquisition for preservation in public institutions.** The Act also addresses other matters connected with or incidental to the protection and conservation of India’s cultural heritage.
● Under this Act, unless the context indicates otherwise, the term “antiquity” includes the following: ○ Any coin, sculpture, painting, epigraph, or other work of art or craftsmanship. ○ Any article, object, or item detached from a building or cave. ○ Any object that illustrates the science, art, crafts, literature, religion, customs, morals, or political life of past ages. ○ Any article or object of historical significance.
● Under the Act a **manuscript, record or document** is considered an antiquity if it has been in existence for **not less than seventy-five years**. The Act clearly provides a **lower age threshold** for manuscripts compared to other antiquities. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Act defines an **“antiquity”** to include **any article, object, or thing** which has been in existence for **not less than one hundred years**. This is the **general age criterion** applicable to most movable antiquities such as sculptures, coins, paintings, etc. **So, statement II is correct**
● The Antiquities and Art Treasures Act, 1972 **does not expressly recognise temple sculptures as living** **entities.** Its primary objective is to regulate the export and trade of antiquities and art treasures and to provide for their compulsory acquisition to ensure their preservation in public institutions. **So, statement** **III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
With reference to the chronology and features
of the Indus Valley Civilisation, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Rakhigarhi and Banawali exhibit a continuous
sequence across the Early, Mature, and Late Harappan phases.
II. 	 Extensive stone-based fortifications and a
tripartite town plan are distinctive features of Dholavira.
III. 	Kalibangan reached its maximum urban
extent and significance during the Late Harappan phase.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indus Valley Civilisation** is broadly divided into **Early Harappan (c. 3300–2600 BCE)**, **Mature** **Harappan (c. 2600–1900 BCE)**, and **Late Harappan (c. 1900–1300 BCE)** phases.
