The Early phase is marked by the emergence of village settlements, regional cultures, and early town planning. The Mature phase represents the height of urbanisation, characterized by well-planned cities, standardized bricks, drainage systems, seals, and extensive trade networks. The Late Harappan phase shows signs of urban decline, regionalisation, reduced standardisation, and the dispersal of populations into smaller settlements.
● **Rakhigarhi and Banawali** show continuity across all three Harappan phases. Excavations at **Rakhigarhi** reveal early settlement layers, mature urban features, and later simplified habitation. **Banawali** also demonstrates gradual changes in town planning and material culture rather than abrupt abandonment, indicating evolutionary continuity. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Dholavira** is notable for its massive **stone-built fortifications** and a clear **tripartite town plan** comprising the citadel, middle town, and lower town. The extensive use of stone, along with large water reservoirs, distinguishes it from brick-built Harappan cities like Harappa and Mohenjo-daro. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **Kalibangan** reached its peak during the **Mature Harappan phase**, with planned streets and fortified areas. After this phase, the site was largely abandoned, likely due to environmental changes affecting the Ghaggar–Hakra river, and therefore does not represent a major Late Harappan settlement. **So, statement** **III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
In the context of recent technological developments
in India, what are ‘DHRUV64’ and ‘THEJAS64’?

options.a:
GPS-aided navigation systems for the Indian
Navy
options.b:
Microprocessors capable of handling strategic
operations and industrial automation
options.c:
Linux-based operating systems designed for
secure government communications
options.d:
Advanced stealth variants of the Dhruv Utility
Helicopter and Tejas Fighter Jet

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **DHRUV64 and THEJAS64 are indigenously developed microprocessors.** The DHRUV64 chip is a fully indigenous microprocessor developed by the Centre for Development of Advanced Computing (CDAC) under the Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MEITY)’s Microprocessor Development Programme.
o According to MEITY, DHRUV64 is part of India’s ecosystem of processors, including SHAKTI from IIT-Madras, AJIT from IIT-Bombay, VIKRAM from the ISRO-Semiconductor Lab, and THEJAS64 from C-DAC (2025). The needs these processors address include strategic operations, control systems in factories, spaceflight systems, and industrial automation. o It can also be used as a platform on which startups, academia, and industry can build and test products on, “without relying on foreign processors”, and can develop prototypes for new system architectures at lower cost.
● **Both are based on the RISC-V architecture**, which is an "open-source" instruction set architecture. RISC-V (pronounced “risk five”) is a set of basic instructions that a processor understands. RISC-V is an open instruction set, which means its core rules are publicly available and anyone can design a chip that follows them without paying a license fee for the instruction set itself. **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology announced the launch of DHRUV64, an indigenous microprocessor.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to nanobodies, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 They can be used as antivenoms and are
much smaller, simpler, and more stable than conventional antibodies.
II. 	 They are found naturally in camelids and
some cartilaginous fish.
III. 	Unlike conventional antibodies, they cannot
be mass produced in bacterial cell culture.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Conventional antibodies** are heterotetrameric (Having four units), consisting of two heavy and two light chains. In contrast, nanobodies are monomeric, comprising only the heavy-chain part of the antibody. Such heavy chain only antibodies, which lack light chains, are **naturally found in camelids** and **cartilaginous fish like sharks** (where they are called Variable New Antigen Receptors).
o **Nanobodies** are highly stable under extreme temperatures and pH levels due to their single-domain structure. They are **used as broad spectrum antivenoms** because they are **less immunogenic**, have a faster distribution phase to reach venom toxins in tissues, and can be engineered to neutralize small toxins that are "invisible" for bulky conventional antibodies. **So, statements I and** **II are correct.**
o The **smaller size of nanobodies** results in improved tissue penetration and ability to penetrate tumor cells hence, this allows for more specific and efficient drug delivery, as well as improved imaging capability. Additionally, nanobodies have been shown to be able to cross the blood brain barrier. These features culminated in the approval of the world’s first nanobody based **drug** (**Caplacizumab**) in 2018, to treat acquired Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (aTTP), a rare and life-threatening blood-clotting disorder.
● **Nanobodies** are encoded by a single gene and do not require complex post translation modifications. They can be expressed with high yields in prokaryotic (Bacterial) systems like **Escherichia coli**. The DNA that codes for the nanobodies is genetically engineered into the genome of bacteriophage viruses. The virus particles express nanobodies on their surface.  The nanobodies that bind most strongly to antigen like snake venom toxins, are selected. These selected nanobodies can now be mass-produced cheaply in bacteria. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Recently a Danish research laboratory founded a broad-spectrum treatment for snake bites based on nanobodies.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** All genetic codons in all living organisms code for the same amino acids.
**Statement II:** Genetic codons are read during the process of translation to determine the order of amino acids in a protein.

options.a:
Both statement I and statement II are correct
and statement II explains statement I.
options.b:
Both statement I and statement-II are correct,
but statement II does **not** explain statement I.
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but statement II is
incorrect.
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but statement II is
correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The genetic code is a set of rules that defines how the four-letter nucleotide sequence of DNA, through transcription into mRNA, is translated into the 20-letter amino acid sequence of proteins. The genetic code consists of **three-nucleotide sequences called codons**, each of which specifies a particular amino acid or a stop signal. The concept of codons was first elucidated by Francis Crick and his colleagues in 1961.
o Nirenberg, Philip Leder, and Hargobind Khorana identified the triplet nature of codon and its corresponding amino acid. There are 64 possible permutations, or combinations, of three-letter nucleotide sequences that can be made from the four nucleotides. Of these 64 codons, 61 represent amino acids, and three are stop signals (UAG, UGA and UAA). o At the heart of genetic codon there are the four nitrogen-containing bases that are a part of DNA: adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C) and thymine (T). Each amino acid in a protein corresponds to a three base long codon on mRNA. For example, a codon consisting of three thymine (TTT) corresponds to the amino acid phenylalanine, while TTA encodes leucine. o The **genetic code is nearly universal**, with few rare variations reported. For example, in human **mitochondria**, the codon **UGA** (a stop codon in human) codes for Tryptophan, while AGA and AGG (normally coding for Arginine) act as Stop codons. In archaea where **TAG** is known to sometimes encode pyrrolysine (Pyl) thus, they code for 21 amino acids instead of the conventional
20. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● In the process of **translation**, the sequence of codons (triplets of nucleotides) on the mRNA is read by ribosomes and tRNA to assemble amino acids in a specific linear sequence, ultimately forming a polypeptide chain or protein. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, Statement–I is not correct and Statement–II is correct.** **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** A new and unusual genetic code in Antarctic microbes is found which yields a rare amino acid.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Check the health and chlorophyll of plants
content.
II. 	 Track pollution or harmful algal blooms in
water bodies.
III. 	Detects underground oil and gas deposits
even in the absence of surface seepage.
IV. 	 Determine the exact chemical composition of
deep ocean water at all depths.
How many of the statements given above are applications of remote sensing satellites?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Remote sensing** is the process of detecting and monitoring the physical characteristics of an area by measuring its reflected and emitted radiation at a distance (typically from satellite or aircraft). **Every** **material has a unique "spectral signature"**. Remote sensing is used to check the health of plants. Healthy leaves are full of chlorophyll, which absorbs red light for photosynthesis and reflects near-infrared light to avoid overheating.
o Scientists use the **normalised difference vegetation index** to determine if a plant is healthy **based** **on its spectral signatures**. If a satellite observes high near-infrared reflection, the crops are healthy. If the reflection of that part of the spectrum drops, the plants might be sick. **So, statement** **I is correct**.
● To map water bodies from space, mainly two complementary techniques are used: **optical indexing**, using reflected sunlight, and **synthetic aperture radar**, using active radio waves. The optical indexing technique depends upon the fact that water reflects visible green light, which is why deep water often looks blue green, but strongly absorbs near-infrared and shortwave infrared light. Muddy water reflects light differently than clear water, and water full of algae has a specific spectral signature. This helps environmentalists track pollution or harmful algal blooms through remote sensing. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Optical and Infrared sensors in remote sensing **cannot see through the Earth's crust**. They can only detect oil and gas indirectly if there is a surface leak (micro-seepage) that affects the soil, kills vegetation, or creates a thermal anomaly. Without surface seepage, **direct detection of deep** **underground deposits via satellite is not currently possible.** **So, statement III is not correct.**
● Remote Sensing is used for **ocean water quality** (OWQ) monitoring and can give insight of physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of oceanic waters. While satellites are excellent for monitoring Sea Surface Temperature (SST) or surface salinity, they cannot "see" through miles of water to determine the chemical composition of the deep ocean or the sea floor.
o Because electromagnetic radiation **cannot penetrate deep into the ocean**; it is absorbed or scattered within the first few meters (the photic zone). **So, statement IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the above applications of remote sensing are correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** ISRO is set to launch the EOS-N1 earth observation satellite aboard PSLV-C62 on January 12, marking its first mission of 2026.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Quantum technology,
consider the following pairs:
Term Description
I. - Scrambling Dispersal of local information across all the other quantum bits
II. - Interference Subatomic particles interact with and influence themselves and other particles
III. - Quantum advantage
Performing a task that is beyond the reach of traditional supercomputers In how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **c**
**Explanation:**
● In quantum technology, **scrambling means** that information stored in one quantum bit does not remain local but quickly spreads across all the other quantum bits (through entanglement) in the system. After scrambling, the original information cannot be traced back to a single qubit, even though it still exists in the system as a whole. This property is important for understanding quantum information flow and quantum security. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● **Interference** is a basic feature of quantum physics in which subatomic particles such as electrons or photons **behave like waves and can interact with themselves as well as with other particles.** When these waves overlap, they can strengthen or weaken each other, affecting the final outcome of a quantum process. This phenomenon is essential for the working of quantum computers and quantum experiments. **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● **Quantum advantage refers to the situation where a quantum computer can perform a specific task** **much faster or more efficiently than a classical computer.** Some problems that would take traditional supercomputers an extremely long time to solve can be handled more effectively using quantum systems. This highlights the practical benefit of quantum technology over classical computing in certain applications.
o A qubit, governed by the principles of quantum mechanics, can exist in a superposition of the states 0 and 1, with specific probabilities revealed upon measurement. While a single qubit yields only one classical bit when measured, quantum computers exploit superposition and entanglement across multiple qubits to process information in ways that are not feasible for classical computers. **So, row III is correctly matched.** **So, in all the above rows the information is correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (c) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Quantum chips:**
● A **quantum computing** chip is the hardware component at the heart of a quantum computer, designed to process and manipulate quantum information. Google has developed its quantum chip named **Willow**.
● **Microsoft** has announced its **Majorana** 1 quantum chip. **IBM** also unveils a new ‘**Nighthawk**’ quantum chip.
**Relevance:** Google reported that its quantum chip, Willow, has achieved quantum advantage.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With with regards to gravitational lensing,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 It occurs when a massive celestial body
causes a curvature of spacetime leading the path of light to bend.
II. 	 It can occur only when the source, the lens,
and the observer are perfectly aligned along a straight line.
III. 	It can be used to directly detect gravitational
waves through spacetime distortions.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gravitational lensing** occurs because massive celestial bodies such as stars, galaxies, or black holes **curve/wrap spacetime around them.** When light from a distant object passes through this curved spacetime, its path bends, making the object appear distorted, magnified, or multiplied. **So, statement I** **is correct.**
● **Perfect alignment** of the source, the lens, and the observer is **not necessary** for gravitational lensing to occur. Exact alignment only produces special features like an **Einstein ring.** In most cases, **even partial** **alignment is enough to cause bending and distortion of light.** **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Gravitational lensing** cannot be used to directly detect gravitational waves. Gravitational waves are detected using highly sensitive instruments such as LIGO and Virgo, which measure tiny ripples in spacetime caused by violent cosmic events. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Astronomers have spotted a rare kind of world drifting through the Milky Way entirely on its own, confirming the existence of a “free-floating” planet nearly 10,000 light-years from Earth using gravitational microlensing.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Significantly enhancing data storage capacity
in hard drives
II. 	 Facilitating energy efficient electronics by
controlling electron spin
III. 	Making nonvolatile memory that retains data
without continuous power
How many of the above are the potential applications of Altermagnetism?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Altermagnetism is a recently discovered type of magnetism in which magnetic moments of electrons** **align in opposite directions following a unique rotated pattern.** This arrangement combines properties of both ferromagnets and antiferromagnets like antiferromagnets; altermagnets have zero net magnetisation, eliminating stray magnetic fields, while like ferromagnets, they allow spin-polarised currents. These characteristics make altermagnets particularly promising for applications in data storage, energy-efficient electronics, and advanced memory technologies.
● **Altermagnets can significantly increase data storage capacity** because their zero net magnetisation prevents stray magnetic fields, which otherwise limit how closely data bits can be packed in conventional hard drives. This property allows storage elements to be positioned more densely, resulting in higher data density and improved speed and efficiency of microelectronic components. **So, point I is correct.**
● Altermagnets enable spin-polarised currents without requiring an external magnetic field, which forms the basis for **energy-efficient spintronic devices**. By leveraging electron spin rather than just charge, these devices can perform computations and process information with lower energy consumption and higher speed, making them useful for both general electronics and advanced computing technologies. **So, point** **II is correct.**
● Altermagnets can also be used to design **non-volatile memory elements**, **which retain stored** **information even when power is switched off. Using electron spin instead of charge to encode data** provides greater stability, faster read/write operations, and reduced energy consumption, making altermagnets highly promising for next-generation memory and computing applications. **So, point III is** **correct.** **So, all of the above are the potential applications of Altermagnetism.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Recently researchers have detected a striking novel behaviour in a class of magnetic material known as altermagnets that could one day revolutionize how we design electronic devices.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
Consider the following organelles:
I. - Mitochondria
II. 	 Nucleus
III. 	 Chloroplast
IV. 	 Nitroplast
How many of the above possess a double membrane and contain their own genetic material (DNA)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mitochondria** are known as the **powerhouses of the cell** due to their role in energy production. **They are** **double-membraned organelles**, with a relatively permeable outer membrane and a highly folded inner membrane forming cristae, which increase the surface area for **adenosine triphosphate (ATP) synthesis**. Mitochondria also possess their own DNA and ribosomes, enabling the synthesis of some of their proteins. **So, point I is correct.**
● The **nucleus is a double membrane-bound organelle that houses the chromosomes.** It is enclosed by a nuclear membrane with pores that regulate the exchange of materials between the nucleus and the cytoplasm. Chromosomes, composed of DNA and proteins, become distinctly visible during cell division and **carry hereditary information** in the form of DNA. In non-dividing cells, DNA exists in a less condensed form as chromatin, and its functional units are called genes. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Chloroplasts** are plastids found in plant cells, usually round, oval, or disc-shaped, and are responsible for the synthesis and storage of food. Their green colour is due to chlorophyll a and b, **which capture light** **energy for photosynthesis, converting it into chemical energy.** Chloroplasts are enclosed by a doublemembraned envelope and, like mitochondria, contain their own DNA and ribosomes, reflecting their semiautonomous nature and supporting the endosymbiotic theory. **So, point III is correct.**
● A nitroplast is a recently identified nitrogen-fixing organelle found in the **unicellular marine eukaryotic** **alga** **Braarudosphaera bigelowii**. It is believed to have originated from an endosymbiotic cyanobacterium
and represents an early stage in the evolution of a new organelle. The nitroplast is the first known organelle capable of fixing atmospheric nitrogen within a eukaryotic cell, thereby addressing the long-standing limitation of eukaryotes, which generally lack the ability to fix nitrogen independently. Similar to mitochondria and chloroplasts, **nitroplasts are enclosed by a double membrane and contain their own** **genetic material,** supporting the endosymbiotic theory of organelle evolution. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, all of the above organelles possess a double membrane and contain their own genetic material (DNA).** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The nitroplast is a newly discovered, groundbreaking organelle that enables marine algae (Braarudosphaera bigelowii) to fix nitrogen directly from the air, a capability previously thought exclusive to bacteria.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
Consider the following processes:
I. 	 Steam Methane Reforming
II. 	 Partial Oxidation
III. 	Autothermal Reforming
IV. 	 Gasification
How many of the above processes can be used to produce syngas?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Syngas or synthesis gas** is a fuel-rich gas mixture used in industrial processes as both an energy source and a raw material for producing chemicals. It is mainly made up of **Hydrogen (H2) and Carbon monoxide (CO)** and can be converted into electricity, fertilizers (like ammonia), methanol, synthetic fuels, and other chemicals **such** **as methanol, ammonia, and synthetic fuels**.  It can be produced through the following processes**:**
● **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** is a process where natural gas (mainly methane) is mixed with very hot steam. When this mixture passes over a special catalyst, such as nickel,  the methane breaks apart and turns into a mixture of synthesis gas. This is the most widely used industrial method to make syngas. **So,** **point I is correct.**
● **Partial Oxidation** involves burning a hydrocarbon fuel, such as natural gas or oil, with a limited amount of oxygen. Because the oxygen is not enough for complete combustion, the fuel only burns partially. This partial burning breaks the fuel into a gaseous mixture known as syngas. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Autothermal Reforming (ATR)** combines both steam reforming and partial oxidation in a single step. Steam and a small amount of oxygen are added together with the hydrocarbon fuel. The heat released from the partial burning helps drive the steam-based reaction. Together, they produce syngas efficiently inside one reactor. **So, point III is correct.**
● Gasification is used mainly for solid or heavy fuels such as coal, biomass, or heavy oils. These materials are heated to very high temperatures with steam, oxygen, or both. Instead of burning completely, the material breaks down into a **combustible gas mixture**. This resulting gas, called **syngas**, primarily contains **hydrogen (H₂), carbon monoxide (CO), and carbon dioxide (CO₂)**. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Greenhouse Gases (GHG), ODS, Related Terms, Global Warming
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
nitrous oxide:
I. 	 Globally, agricultural use of nitrogen based fertilizers constitutes the largest anthropogenic source of its emissions.
II. 	 It is the most significant ozone layer depleting
substance being emitted into the atmosphere.
III. 	It is approximately 270 times more potent
than carbon dioxide in terms of warming the planet.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **d**
**Explanation:**
● **Globally, nitrous oxide is** primarily emitted from agricultural practices such as the use of synthetic fertilizers and manure. Agricultural nitrous oxide emissions are emissions produced through fertilizer use (synthetic and animal manure), animal waste management, agricultural waste burning (nonenergy, onsite), and savanna burning. Crops only take up, on average, about half of the nitrogen they get from fertilizers. Much of the applied fertilizer runs off into waterways, or gets broken down by microbes in the soil, releasing the potent greenhouse gas nitrous oxide into the atmosphere. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Nitrous oxide** is currently the most significant ozone-layer depleting substance being emitted into the atmosphere. When it reaches the stratosphere, it reacts with solar radiation to form nitrogen oxides (NOx),
which destroy ozone molecules. Unlike CFCs, it is not currently regulated by the Montreal Protocol. **So, statement II is correct.**
o The Assessment shows that proactively tackling N₂O would also support the continued recovery of the ozone layer, helping to avoid a future where much of the global population is exposed to harmful UV levels.
● **Nitrous oxide** is approximately 270 (around 273 according to Sixth Assessment Report of Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) times more potent than carbon dioxide in terms of **Global Warming Potential,** and currently responsible for approximately 10% of net global warming since the industrial revolution. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
o Global Warming Potential, measures how much heat a greenhouse gas traps in the atmosphere compared to carbon dioxide over a specific time (usually 100 years), with carbon dioxide as the baseline (GWP=1). **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Findings from the **Global Nitrous Oxide Assessment (N₂O) by UNEP** warn that without urgent action on rising N₂O emissions, there is no viable pathway to limiting global warming to 1.5°C and providing tangible tools to reduce emissions by more than 40% from current levels.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ramsar Sites, World Heritage Sites, UNESCO Heritage Sites, WHS, Biodiversity Heritage Sites, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following countries:
I. 	 Peru
II. 	 Colombia
III. 	Venezuela
IV. 	 Ecuador
V. 	 Bolivia
VI. 	Chile
How many of the above share the Amazon rainforest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Amazon rainforest** is the **largest tropical rainforest in the world**, covering about **5.5 to 6 million** **square kilometres** of the Amazon Basin in **northern South America**. It is characterised by **dense** **vegetation, rich biodiversity, and extensive river networks**, with the Amazon River and its many tributaries running through much of this vast forested region. The distribution of the rainforest is governed by natural geography rather than political borders, and **Brazil contains the largest share**, holding about 60 % of the forest area.
● The rainforest spans **eight countries and one overseas territory of France**: **Brazil, Peru, Colombia,** **Bolivia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Guyana, Suriname, and French Guiana (an overseas territory of** **France)**. All these countries include parts of the Amazon biome within their boundaries, with **Peru,** **Colombia, Bolivia, Venezuela, and Ecuador each having significant portions** beyond Brazil’s dominant share.  **So, points I, II, III, IV and V are correct.**
○ Because of its enormous size and ecological importance, the Amazon is home to **incredible** **biodiversity and many Indigenous communities**. It plays a vital role in **regional and global** **climate regulation**, stores large amounts of carbon, and supports countless plant and animal species. Conservation of this rainforest is crucial for ecological balance and combating climate change.
● On the other hand, **Chile** does not have any part of the Amazon rainforest. Its long, narrow geography along the western edge of South America, bordered by the Andes and the Pacific Ocean, results in ecosystems that are largely desert, Mediterranean, or temperate forest, but not tropical rainforest. **So, point** **VI is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Recently, in the Brazilian Amazon, the Belém Summit opens ahead of the 30th United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP30). World leaders convened here in the days leading up to the COP so that we can all commit to acting with the urgency that the climate crisis demands.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Consider the following ecological/evolutionary
factors:
I. 	 Mutation
II. 	 Abundance of resources
III. 	Continuous gene flow
IV. 	 Reproductive isolation
How many of the above factors are responsible for speciation?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **b**
**Explanation:**
● **Speciation** is the evolutionary process where new, distinct species **form from an ancestral one**, marked by genetic divergence and the development of reproductive isolation, meaning they can no longer interbreed to produce fertile offspring.
● **Mutation refers to an inheritable change in the DNA sequence of an organism. It introduce new** **traits and alleles into a population's gene pool.** In the process of forming new species, mutations are essential because they provide the necessary divergence for populations to develop distinct characteristics over time. **So, point I is correct.**
● **An abundance of resources generally leads to population growth and stability.** Speciation is more frequently driven by resource competition, environmental pressure, or the colonization of new, diverse niches where resources are different, forcing adaptation. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Continuous gene flow** serves as a homogenizing force that maintains the genetic integrity of a species by facilitating the exchange of alleles between disparate populations. This constant genetic recombination prevents the accumulation of significant divergence, thereby preserving a unified gene pool. **So, point III** **is not correct.**
● **Reproductive** isolation constitutes the fundamental mechanism of speciation, acting as a definitive **barrier to gene flow** between populations. Whether initiated through **allopatric means (geographical** **barriers) or sympatric means (biological divergence),** these isolating mechanisms prevent successful hybridization. By establishing a state of genetic independence, reproductive isolation facilitates the accumulation of distinct mutations and adaptive traits, eventually leading to the formation of a new, distinct species. **So, point IV is correct.** **So, only two of the above ecological/evolutionary factors contribute to speciation.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
“These microscopic, single-celled organisms are
found in diverse aquatic environments ranging from oceans to freshwater lakes. Characterized by unique cell walls made of silica, they exhibit an extraordinary variety of intricate shapes and patterns. They serve as the bedrock of aquatic food webs and are prolific producers, accounting for nearly 25% of the Earth’s total oxygen production.” The above description best refers to which one of the following?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **a**
**Explanation:**
● **Diatoms** are **microscopic unicellular algae** that have a silica shell resistant to acids, heat, or body decomposition. They live in colonies, grow in abundance in fresh and salt waters, and their “skeletons” are widespread in soils. They are famous for their intricate, glass-like **cell walls (frustules) made of silica**, forming beautiful patterns and acting as durable "shells".
o They are major **photosynthetic** **organisms (phytoplankton)** and **form the foundation of aquatic** **food webs and are major contributors to global oxygen production, nearly one-fourth of the** **oxygen production.** Their silica deposits create diatomaceous earth, used in industry, and their fossilized shells help scientists study past climates.
● **Bryophytes** are a group of plants that include mosses, liverworts and hornworts. Bryophytes are nonvascular, non-flowering land plants. **They are known as the "amphibians of the plant kingdom"** because they need water for reproduction. They lack true roots, stems, and leaves, absorbing water through their surface, which limits their size, and are typically found in moist, shady environments.
● **Mycorrhiza** is a symbiotic fungus and has mutualistic association with the roots of vascular plants, in
which both benefit from each other. The host plant helps in fungal growth and reproduction. On the other hand, mycorrhizal fungi help in improving the nutritional status of the plant by providing minerals, water absorption, disease resistance, and enhancing plant growth.
● **Copepods** are a class of small crustaceans that are widespread and naturally abundant in all aquatic ecosystems as a key link between primary producers and higher predators. **Therefore**, **option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** A team of Indian and international researchers discovered a new species of Diatoms in Udupi.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Movement of Ocean Water (Tides, Waves, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Recently, the vast brown seaweed known as the
‘Great Atlantic Sargassum Belt (GASB)’ was in the news. In this context, which of the following is **not** a factor contributing to this phenomenon?

options.a: Anthropogenic nutrient enrichment like phosphorus and nitrogen from agricultural runoff.
options.b:
Fine dust blown from the Sahara Desert
enriching the ocean with iron.
options.c: Anomalous rise in sea surface temperatures.
options.d:
Strengthening of cold, oxygen-rich upwelling
currents

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **d**
**Explanation:** The **Great Atlantic Sargassum Belt (GASB)** is a massive bloom of macroalgae that **stretches from West Africa to** **the Gulf of Mexico.** The factors fueling its growth are primarily linked to the following reasons:
● In recent decades, the Atlantic Ocean has received a **growing amount of nutrients from land-based** **sources.** Fertilizers used in agriculture, untreated wastewater, and urban runoff flow into rivers such as the Amazon, Congo, and Mississippi.
● Wind-blown **dust from the Sahara Desert** **contains iron and other minerals.** When this dust settles over the Atlantic, it provides essential micronutrients that stimulate the rapid growth of Sargassum.
● Shifts in the North Atlantic Oscillation altered **wind directions and current movements, transporting** **Sargassum from its usual home in the Sargasso Sea** **into warmer, nutrient-rich tropical waters.** These new conditions allowed the seaweed to grow at an unprecedented rate.
● **Sargassum blooms are associated with warmer watercurrents.** Furthermore, while the algae produce oxygen through photosynthesis while alive, when the massive mats die and decompose near coasts, they create hypoxic (low oxygen) "dead zones" that kill marine life. They do not depend on "oxygenated coldwater currents" for their expansion. **Therefore**, **option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity Basics (Types of Biodiversity)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Food and Agriculture
Organization (FAO)’s Global Forest Resources Assessment 2025, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Brazil has the largest area under tropical
forests in the world.
II. 	 Among continents, only Asia recorded an
increase in forest area between 1990 and 2025.
III. 	India recorded the highest annual net gain in
tropical forest area.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **FAO’s Global Forest Resources Assessment (GFRA) 2025** provides updated data on **forest area, types,** **biodiversity, and management** worldwide. It tracks trends in **deforestation, forest degradation, and** **sustainable forest management**, helping countries monitor progress toward **SDGs and global forest goals**.
● According to it India has achieved a milestone in global forest statistics by advancing to the **9th position** worldwide in terms of **total forest area** In the previous assessment, in 2020, India was ranked 10th.
● At the global level, the GFRA 2025 estimates the world’s total forest cover at approximately 4.14 billion hectares, which constitutes about 32% of the Earth’s land area, translating to nearly 0.5 hectares of forest per capita. India accounts for around **72.7 million hectares of forest area**, representing roughly **2% of** **the world’s total forest cover.**
● **Brazil** possesses the **largest tropical forest area globally,** with approximately **486 million hectares** **under forest cover.** A significant portion of this, nearly 60%, is contributed by the **Amazon Rainforest,** often referred to as the “lungs of the Earth.” The Amazon plays a vital role in carbon sequestration and supports unparalleled biodiversity, sustaining millions of plant and animal species. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **More than half of the world’s forest area** (**about 54%)** is concentrated in just five countries: **Russia,** **Brazil, Canada, the United States and China**. During the period 2015–2025, **China recorded the** **highest annual net gain in forest area,** averaging 1.69 million hectares per year, followed by the Russian Federation with approximately 0.94 million hectares and **India with around 0.19 million hectares** **annually. So, statement III is not correct.**
● The assessment highlights that **Asia is the only region to have experienced a net increase in forest area** **between 1990 and 2025,** driven primarily by afforestation and reforestation efforts in China and India. **So, statement II is correct.**
● At the global level, the annual rate of net forest loss has declined by more than half, from 10.7 million hectares in the 1990s to about 4.12 million hectares during 2015–2025, indicating gradual progress in forest conservation efforts. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** According to PIB GFRA 2025 Ranks India 9th Globally in Total Forest Area, 3rd in Net Annual Forest Area Gain

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
With reference to rangelands, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 They collectively account for more than half
of the Earth’s terrestrial surface.
II. 	 They store a greater proportion of carbon
below ground compared to forest ecosystems.
III. 	The United Nations has designated 2026 as
the International Year of Rangelands and Pastoralists.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rangeland,** are any extensive area of land that is occupied by native herbaceous or shrubby vegetation
which is grazed by domestic or wild herbivores.  **Rangelands** cover **more than half of the Earth’s land** **surface** and sustain over 500 million people who rely on pastoralism for their livelihoods. These diverse landscapes, spanning **grasslands, savannahs, deserts and shrublands**, play a crucial role in biodiversity conservation, ecosystem regulation and climate resilience. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Unlike forests, where a large share of carbon is stored in above-ground biomass, **rangelands store** **proportionally more carbon in soils and root systems.** This below-ground storage is less vulnerable to disturbances such as fire and deforestation, making rangelands a more stable carbon sink. Grasslands and
shrublands together store a significant share of the Earth s terrestrial carbon, with grasslands alone accounting for roughly **25 – 34 % of global terrestrial carbon stocks**, much of it in below-ground soil organic carbon, and contribute substantially to annual ecosystem carbon sequestration, underscoring their critical role in climate change mitigation.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● To draw much-needed global attention to rangelands and the communities that depend on them, the **United** **Nations** has declared **2026 as the International Year of Rangelands and Pastoralists (IYRP).** The initiative, led by **Mongolia** and supported by a broad coalition of stakeholders, seeks to raise awareness, promote responsible investments and inform policy frameworks aimed at protecting rangelands and sustaining pastoralist livelihoods. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The **United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD)**, as the principal international body addressing land degradation, is committed to ensuring that rangelands receive the visibility, policy focus, and on-ground action they urgently require. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Indian polity:
I. 	 In matters in the Concurrent List where
Parliament has legislated, executive power vests exclusively in the Union Government.
II. 	 The President of India may entrust Union
executive functions to a State with the consent of the State Government.
III. 	Parliament can confer Union legislative
powers on States through legislation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **distribution of powers between the Union and the states** is governed by **Part XI of the** **Constitution of India**.
● The **executive power of the Union** is defined in **Article 73**. Under **Article 73(1)(a)**, the **executive power** **of the Union extends to all matters on which Parliament has the power to legislate**. However, In respect of matters on which both the Parliament and the state legislatures have power of legislation i.e., the **subjects enumerated in the Concurrent List, the executive power rests with the states except** **when a Constitutional provision or a parliamentary law specifically confers it on the Centre**. Therefore, a law on a concurrent subject, though enacted by the Parliament, is to be executed by the states except when the Constitution or the Parliament has directed otherwise. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Article 258(1)** provides that the **President of India may entrust Union executive functions to a State** **Government or its officers, with the consent of that State**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Parliament cannot**, by an ordinary law, **transfer its exclusive legislative competence over Union List** **subjects to State Legislatures**, as legislative powers are constitutionally demarcated. **So, statement III** **is not correct.**
○ Although the Constitution empowers the Parliament to make laws on any matter enumerated in the State List under the following five extraordinary circumstances:
■ When Rajya Sabha passes a resolution (Article 249), ■ When States make a request (Article 252), ■ To implement international agreement ■ During President’s Rule ■ During a National Emergency **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the original jurisdiction of the
Supreme Court of India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 Article 131 of the Constitution of India
empowers the Supreme Court to decide intergovernmental disputes involving questions of law only.
II. 	 It extends to disputes between the Union and
a Public Sector Undertaking (PSU).
III. 	It extends to disputes arising out of any pre-
Constitution treaty, agreement, or covenant.
IV. 	 It extends to the adjustment of certain
expenses and pensions between the Centre and the States.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court** refers to cases that can be brought **directly before it in** **the first instance**, without going through any lower court.
● Under **Article 131**, the Supreme Court’s original jurisdiction extends to disputes between the **Centre and** **States or between States**, but the dispute must involve a question of **law or fact** on which the existence or extent of a **legal right** depends. **So, statement I is not correct**.
● The original jurisdiction under Article 131 applies only to **federal units** (the Union and States). A **Public** **Sector Undertaking (PSU)** is not a federal unit but a government-owned company, so disputes between the Union and a PSU do **not** fall under Article 131. **So, statement II is not correct**.
● The Constitution expressly **excludes disputes arising out of pre-Constitution treaties, agreements,** **covenants, sanads, etc.** from the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Such matters are instead covered under the Court’s **advisory jurisdiction** under **Article 143**. **So, statement III is not correct**.
● The Constitution specifically provides that matters relating to the **adjustment of certain expenses and** **pensions between the Centre and the States** are **excluded from the original jurisdiction** of the Supreme Court. Hence, such matters do not fall within Article 131. **So, statement IV is not correct**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
In which of the following matters is the Legislative
Council placed on an unequal footing with the Legislative Assembly in India?
I. 	 Approval of Ordinances issued by the
Governor
II. 	 Participation in the discussion on the State
Budget
III. 	Ratification of a Constitutional Amendment
Bill
IV. 	 Moving or passing a No-Confidence Motion
against the Council of Ministers, when the Chief Minister is a member of the Legislative Council
V. 	 Enlargement of the jurisdiction of the State
Public Service Commission.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In a **bicameral State Legislature**, the **Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad)** is the **Upper House**, while the **Legislative Assembly (Vidhan Sabha)** is the **popular and more powerful Lower House**.
● The Constitution places the **Legislative Council (LC)** in a subordinate position to the **Legislative** **Assembly (LA)** in several key matters.
● The **approval of Ordinances issued by the Governor** is listed under areas where the **Council and** **Assembly are equal**. **So, point I is not correct**.
● The **Council can discuss the budget on equal footing with the LA but cannot vote on the Demands** **for Grants**, which is an **exclusive privilege of the Assembly**. **So, point II is not correct**.
● The **Council** has no effective role in the **ratification of a Constitutional Amendment Bill**. Here also, the will of the **Assembly** prevails. **So,** **point III is correct**.
● The **Council cannot pass a No-Confidence Motion** even if the Chief Minister is a member of the Upper House of the State Legislature, because the **Council of Ministers is collectively responsible only to the** **Assembly**. **So, point IV is correct**.
● In the case of **Enlargement of the jurisdiction of the State Public Service Commission**  the Council and Assembly have **equal powers**. **So, point V is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts about the Legislative Council:**
● The **Legislative Council is a permanent House**, but its **existence depends on the will of the** **Legislative Assembly**, which can recommend its abolition.
● It is described as a **“dilatory” or advisory chamber**, not a powerful revising body like the Rajya Sabha.
● The **Council of Ministers is responsible only to the Assembly**, not to the Council.
● In the following matters, the powers and status of the **Council** are unequal to that of the **Assembly**: ○ A **Money Bill** can be introduced only in the **Assembly** and not in the **Council**. ○ The **Council** cannot amend or reject a **Money Bill**. It must return the bill to the **Assembly** within **14 days**, either with **recommendations** or without **recommendations**. ○ The **Assembly** may **accept or reject** all or any of the **recommendations** of the **Council**. In both cases, the **Money Bill** is deemed to have been passed by both Houses.
○ The final power to decide whether a bill is a **Money Bill** is vested in the **Speaker of the** **Assembly**. ○ The final power of passing an **Ordinary Bill** also lies with the **Assembly**. The **Council** can only **delay** the bill for a maximum of **four months, three months** in the first instance and **one** **month** in the second instance. ○ If an **Ordinary Bill** originating in the **Council** is **rejected** by the **Assembly**, the bill **lapses**. ○ The **Council** does not participate in the election of the **President of India** or in the election of **State representatives to the Rajya Sabha**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
The President of India s power to make regulations
for the “peace, progress and good government” of
certain Union Territories is subject to which of the
following conditions?

options.a:
It can be exercised for Puducherry only when
its Legislative Assembly is dissolved or
suspended.
options.b:
It can be used to repeal or amend any Act
of Parliament applicable to the Union
Territory.
options.c:
It is a discretionary power not requiring
the advice of the Union Council of
Ministers.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **administration of Union Territories** is governed by **Articles 239 to 241** **(Part VIII)** of the Constitution.
● Every Union Territory is administered by the **President of India acting through an Administrator**, who is an **agent of the President** and not a constitutional head like a Governor.
● Even where a Union Territory has a **Legislative Assembly and Council of Ministers** (as in **Puducherry,** **Delhi and Jammu & Kashmir**), the **supreme control of the President and Parliament continues**.
● The Constitution gives the **President special law-making power** for certain Union Territories.
● The President can make **regulations for the “peace, progress and good government”** of **Andaman and** **Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep, Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu and Ladakh**. These regulations have the **same force and effect as an Act of Parliament** and can **repeal or amend any Act** **of Parliament** applicable to those Union Territories.
● In the case of **Puducherry**, the President can also make such regulations, but **only when its Legislative** **Assembly is suspended or dissolved**.
● The President acts **as part of the Union executive**, and the administration of Union Territories is carried out by the President **acting through administrators**. The Constitution does **not** say that this is a **discretionary power** exercised without the aid and advice of the **Union Council of Ministers**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Finance Commission of
India (FCI), consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is a quasi-judicial body whose
recommendations are binding on the Union
Government to maintain fiscal federalism.
II. 	 The qualifications of the Chairman and
members of the Commission are determined
by the Parliament.
III. 	Income Distance has historically carried the
highest weight in the horizontal devolution
formula to promote fiscal equalisation among
States.
IV. 	 The Sixteenth Finance Commission has
been tasked with reviewing the present
arrangements for financing Disaster
Management initiatives.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Finance Commission** is a **constitutional body** established under **Article 280** of the Constitution to recommend the principles governing **Centre–State financial relations**.
● The Finance Commission is **quasi-judicial** in character, but its **recommendations are only advisory**. The Constitution does **not make them binding** on the Union Government. **So, statement I is not correct**.
● The **composition and qualifications** of the Finance Commission are provided in the Constitution in broad terms, but the **power to determine detailed qualifications and the manner of selection is given to** **Parliament**. Accordingly, under the **Finance Commission (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1951**, Parliament has laid down qualifications such as experience in public affairs, finance, economics, or eligibility to be a High Court judge. **So, statement II is correct**.
● In the **horizontal devolution formula**, **Income Distance** has historically carried the **highest weight** to promote **fiscal equalisation** among States. For example, in the **15th Finance Commission**, ‘Income Distance’ had a **weight of 45%**, making it the most important criterion. **So, statement III is correct**.
● The task of **reviewing the present arrangements for financing Disaster Management initiatives** was given to the **Sixteenth Finance Commission**, as per its **Terms of Reference.** It has been specifically mandated to review funds constituted under the **Disaster Management Act, 2005** and make recommendations. **So, statement IV is correct**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Sixteenth Finance Commission has submited its report for 2026-31 to the President of India.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following offences:
I. 	 Offences related to human trafficking
II. 	 Offences related to prohibited arms under the
Arms Act, 1959
III. 	Cyber-terrorism and offences under the
Explosive Substances Act, 1908
IV. 	 Investigation of offences committed against
Indian citizens or Indian interests outside India
V. 	 Economic offences involving bank fraud and
money laundering
VI. 	Offences relating to taxation and customs
duty evasion
How many of the above fall within the jurisdiction
of the National Investigation Agency (NIA)?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Investigation Agency (NIA)** is India’s **central counter-terrorism law-enforcement** **agency**, established under the **NIA Act, 2008**, and its powers were significantly expanded by the **NIA** **(Amendment) Act, 2019**.
● After the 2019 amendment, the NIA is specifically empowered to investigate **human trafficking and** **circulation of counterfeit currency**. **So, point I is correct**.
● The Amendment Act also brought within NIA’s jurisdiction offences relating to the **manufacture or sale** **of prohibited arms under the Arms Act, 1959**. **So, point II is correct**.
● The NIA has always been empowered to investigate **terrorist acts, bomb blasts, and offences under the** **Explosive Substances Act, 1908** and the 2019 amendment explicitly added **cyber-terrorism** to its mandate. **So, point III is correct**.
● Under the **NIA (Amendment) Act, 2019**, the NIA’s jurisdiction was extended to **scheduled offences** **committed outside India against Indian citizens or Indian interests**. **So, point IV is correct**.
● However, **ordinary economic offences such as bank fraud and money laundering** fall under agencies like the **CBI, ED, or SFIO**, unless they are linked to **terror financing**. **So, point V is not correct**.
● **Taxation and customs duty evasion** are dealt with by revenue and enforcement agencies and are **not** **scheduled offences under the NIA Act** unless connected to terrorism. **So, point VI is not correct**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About National Investigation Agency (NIA):**
● The **NIA** was established in **2009** under the **National Investigation Agency Act, 2008** after the **26/11** **Mumbai terror attacks**.
● It functions under the **Ministry of Home Affairs** and is headed by a **Director-General**.
● It has **concurrent jurisdiction** with States to investigate offences affecting the **sovereignty, security** **and integrity of India**.
● The **NIA (Amendment) Act, 2019** expanded its mandate to include **human trafficking, counterfeit** **currency, cyber-terrorism, prohibited arms and explosives**.
● The NIA acts as India’s **principal federal agency for counter-terrorism and national security-** **related investigations**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.microTopic: Directive Principles of State Policy DPSP
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Constitution of India,
consider the following pairs:
Part Subject
I. - Part II Citizenship
II. - Part XI Relations between the Union and the States
III. - Part XVI Special Provisions relating to Certain Classes
IV. - Part XX Official language
Which of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Constitution of India, adopted in 1950, originally consisted of 395 Articles, 22 Parts, and 8 Schedules. Over time, through amendments, its scope has expanded to address emerging needs of governance and society. Today, the Constitution contains 448 Articles, 25 Parts, and 12 Schedules.
● **Parts of the Constitution of India:** ○ **Part I- The Union and its Territory:** Defines India as a Union of States and regulates the organisation of states and territories. ○ **Part II- Citizenship:** Deals with citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution. **So, pair I** **is correctly matched.** ○ **Part III- Fundamental Rights:** Guarantees Fundamental Rights such as equality, freedom, and life and personal liberty. ○ **Part IV- Directive Principles of State Policy:** Contains non-justiciable principles aimed at establishing a welfare state. ○ **Part IVA- Fundamental Duties:** Lists the Fundamental Duties of citizens under Article 51A. ○ **Part V- The Union:** Deals with the Executive, Legislature, and Judiciary of the Union. ○ **Part VI- The States:** Deals with the Executive, Legislature, and Judiciary of the States. ○ **Part VII- Repealed:** Originally dealt with Part B States and was repealed by the 7th Amendment Act, 1956. ○ **Part VIII- Union Territories:** Deals with the administration of Union Territories. ○ **Part IX- Panchayats:** Contains provisions relating to Panchayati Raj Institutions. ○ **Part IXA- Municipalities:** Deals with Urban Local Bodies. ○ **Part IXB- Co-operative Societies:** Contains provisions relating to Co-operative Societies. ○ **Part X- Scheduled and Tribal Areas:** Contains provisions relating to Scheduled Areas and Tribal Areas. ○ **Part XI- Relations between the Union and the States:** Deals with legislative and administrative relations.  **So, pair II is correctly matched.** ○ **Part XII- Finance, Property, Contracts and Suits:** Deals with financial relations, government property, and legal proceedings. ○ **Part XIII- Trade, Commerce and Intercourse:** Ensures freedom of trade and commerce within India. ○ **Part XIV- Services under the Union and the States:** Deals with civil services. ○ **Part XIVA- Tribunals:** Contains provisions relating to Administrative and other Tribunals. ○ **Part XV- Elections:** Deals with elections and the Election Commission. ○ **Part XVI — Special Provisions relating to Certain Classes:** Provides safeguards for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and Anglo-Indians. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** ○ **Part XVII- Official Language:** Deals with the official language of the Union and States. ○ **Part XVIII- Emergency Provisions:** Contains provisions relating to National, State, and Financial Emergencies. ○ **Part XIX- Miscellaneous:** Contains miscellaneous constitutional provisions. ○ **Part XX- Amendment of the Constitution:** Prescribes the procedure for constitutional amendments under Article 368.  **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** ○ **Part XXI- Temporary, Transitional and Special Provisions:** Contains special and transitional provisions for certain states. ○ **Part XXII- Short Title, Commencement, Authoritative Text in Hindi and Repeals:** Deals with the short title, commencement, authoritative Hindi text, and repeals. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements :
**Statement I:** The provisions of Part IXB of the Constitution of India relating to State Cooperative Societies were struck down by the Supreme Court of India.
**Statement II:** Cooperative societies fall exclusively within the State List in the Seventh Schedule to the Constitution of India.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Part IXB of the Constitution**, introduced by the **97th Constitutional Amendment**, laid down detailed provisions governing the **functioning, management and audit of cooperative societies**.
● However, the Supreme Court struck down Part IXB **in so far as it applied to State cooperative societies**, because it altered the legislative powers of States without following the mandatory constitutional procedure. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Cooperative societies (other than multi-State cooperatives)** fall within the **State List of the Seventh** **Schedule**, and hence are within the **exclusive legislative domain of State Legislatures**.
○ **State List (List II), Entry 32**: Governs cooperative societies whose objects are confined to a single state. ○ **Union List (List I), Entry 44**: Governs Multi-State Cooperative Societies (those with objects not confined to one state).  **So, statement II is not correct.**
● **State legislatures are empowered** to regulate crucial aspects such as: ○ maintenance and audit of accounts, ○ qualifications and appointment of auditors, ○ timelines for audit, and ○ placing audit reports before the State Legislature. **So, statement I is correct but statement II is incorrect** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About the Part IXB and Cooperative Societies:**
● **Part IXB** was inserted into the Constitution by the **97th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2011** to strengthen democratic, professional and autonomous functioning of cooperative societies.
● It laid down constitutional provisions on elections, audit, management, supersession of boards, and accountability of cooperatives.
● **Parliament has power** over **multi-State cooperative societies**, while **State Legislatures** have power over **State cooperative societies**.
● **Part IXB continues to apply to Multi-State Cooperative Societies**, which fall under **Parliament’s** **legislative competence**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the qualifications for election as President of India:
I. 	 He must be at least twenty-five years old.
II. 	 He must be a citizen of India by birth.
III. 	He must be qualified to be elected as a member
of the Lok Sabha.
IV. 	 A sitting Vice-President is eligible to contest
the election for the office.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Articles 52 to 78** of **Part V of the Constitution** deal with the **Union Executive**, which consists of the **President, Vice-President, Prime Minister, Council of Ministers, and the Attorney General of India**.
● The **President** is the **head of the Indian State**, the **first citizen of India**, and a **symbol of unity, integrity,** **and solidarity** of the nation.
● The Constitution requires that the **President be a citizen of India**; unlike the United States, it does not restrict the office to **natural-born citizens**, and even **naturalised citizens** are eligible. **So, point II is not** **correct.**
● He/she should have completed **35 years (not 25 years) of age**. **So, point I is not correct.**
● He/ she should be **qualified for election as a member of the Lok Sabha. So, point III is correct.**
● He /she **should not hold any office of profit** under the Union government or any state government or any local authority or any other public authority.
● A **sitting President or Vice-President, a State Governor, or a Union or State Minister** is **not** **considered to be holding an office of profit** and is therefore eligible to contest.  **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **nomination** of a presidential candidate must be supported by **at least 50 electors as proposers** **and 50 as seconders**, and each candidate must deposit **₹15,000** in the **Reserve Bank of India**. This deposit is **forfeited if the candidate fails to secure one-sixth of the votes polled**.
● Before **1997**, only **10 proposers and 10 seconders** and a **₹2,500 deposit** were required, but these were increased to **discourage non-serious candidates**.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Four Labour Codes in India:
I. 	 The definition of wages has been standardised
across labour codes.
II. 	 Aggregators are required to contribute 4-5%
of their annual turnover to a social security fund for gig and platform workers.
III. 	A trade union must secure at least 75%
worker membership to be recognised as the sole negotiating union.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Government of India has recently announced the implementation of four new Labour Codes to simplify and streamline labour laws in India.
○ The Government has consolidated 29 labour laws into four comprehensive Labour Codes. The four Labour Codes include the **Code on Wages, 2019; the Industrial Relations Code, 2020; the** **Code on Social Security, 2020** and **the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions** **Code, 2020.**
● A **single definition of wages applies across all four Labour Codes**. The same definition applies uniformly for statutory calculations. The definition of “Wages” covers all remuneration, whether by way of salary, allowances or otherwise. These include Basic pay, Dearness allowance and Retaining allowance, if any. **If the allowances (except gratuity and retrenchment compensation) exceed 50% of all** **remuneration, the excess amount shall be added back to wages. So, statement I is correct.**
● Under the Social Security Code, **aggregators are now required to contribute 1-2% of their annual** **turnover** (capped at 5% of payments made or payable to gig/platform workers) **to a Social Security** **Fund.** The fund **finances a range of welfare schemes for Gig workers and Platform workers.** Earlier, these workers bore all risks themselves, with no obligation on aggregators to contribute to their welfare. **So, statement II is not correct.**
○ “Aggregator” is a digital intermediary or a marketplace for a buyer or user of a service to connect with the seller or the service provider.
● According to the Industrial Relations Code: ○ If there is only one trade union functioning in an industrial establishment, the employer is required to recognise such trade union as the sole negotiating union of the workers. ○ In the case of multiple trade unions, **the union representing at least 51% of the workers on the** **muster roll of that industrial establishment will be recognised by the employer as the sole** **negotiating union**. **So, statement III is not correct.**
■ In case this threshold is not met, the Negotiating Council shall be constituted, comprising representatives of all trade unions with at least 20% membership. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** The Government Announces Implementation of Four Labour Codes to Simplify and Streamline Labour Laws

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the PM-SETU Scheme:
I. 	 It is a Central Sector Scheme.
II. 	 It is primarily aimed at the upgradation of
the Government Industrial Training Institutes
(ITIs).
III. 	It will be implemented through the public-
private partnership (PPP) model.
IV. 	 It has financial support from the Asian
Development Bank and the World Bank.
Which of the statements given above are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The PM-SETU (Prime Minister Skilling and Employability Transformation through Upgraded ITIs) scheme was approved by the Union Cabinet in May 2025 with a total budget of ₹60,000 crore. It was officially launched by the Prime Minister on October 4, 2025, to fill existing gaps in the ITI system by making training more relevant to the modern job market.
● **The PM-SETU is a Centrally Sponsored Scheme** launched to strengthen India's vocational training ecosystem. The scheme’s total outlay is ₹60,000 crore **(Central share ₹30,000 crore; State share** **₹20,000 crore; and Industry share₹10,000 crore)** over a five-year period. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● PM-SETU aims to **modernise the ITI ecosystem by upgrading one thousand government ITIs** under a hub-and-spoke model. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ Under PM SETU, the Government plans to upgrade 1,000 Government ITIs using a "hub-andspoke" model, where 200 main "hub" ITIs will each support approximately four "spoke" ITIs with high-tech machinery and equipment. ○ Additionally, the scheme will improve five National Skill Training Institutes (NSTIs) located in Bhubaneshwar, Chennai, Hyderabad, Kanpur, and Ludhiana to create global Centres of Excellence.
● **PM-SETU will be implemented through the public-private partnership (“PPP”) mode**l. A unique feature of PM-SETU is its industry-led governance. Each upgraded ITI will be **managed by a Special** **Purpose Vehicle (SPV), which is a partnership where industry leaders hold 51% ownership and the** **government holds 49%.** This co-investment model allows companies to lead the transformation while receiving up to 83% in government co-funding. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The scheme has co-financing to the extent of 50% of the Central share by the **Asian Development Bank** **and the World Bank,** equally, as a result-based loan. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship invites Industry to lead Upgradation of ITIs under PM-SETU Scheme

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Balance of Payments (BoP)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Payments Regulatory Board,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is a six-member body that has replaced
the Board for Regulation and Supervision of
Payment and Settlement Systems (BPSS).
II. 	 The Governor of the Reserve Bank of India is
the ex officio Chairman of this Board.
III. 	It is legally mandated to meet at least four
times in a financial year.
IV. 	 It is responsible for the regulation of both
electronic and non-electronic payment
systems.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)** has constituted a six-member Payments Regulatory Board (PRB) to oversee the functioning of payment systems in the country. **The new board derives its authority from** **the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007 (PSS Act, 2007).**
○ The **PSS Act, 2007,** provides for the **regulation and supervision of payment systems in India** and **designates the Reserve Bank of India** (Reserve Bank of India) as the authority for that purpose and all related matters. The RBIshall exercise the powers, perform the functions and discharge the duties conferred on it under the PSS Act, 2007 through a Board to be known as the “Payments Regulatory Board”.
● It is a **six-member body** comprising 3 members from the RBI and **3 Government nominees**. The PRB has **replaced the Board for Regulation and Supervision of Payment and Settlement Systems (BPSS),** a committee of the RBI’s Central Board. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ **The RBI Governor is its ex-****officio** **chairman. So, statement II is correct.** ○ The two other RBI representatives on the PRB are the **Deputy Governor** and the one officer of the RBI nominated by the Central Board of the RBI (Ex officio Member).
○ The Board comprises three nominees from the Central Government.
● The **principal legal adviser of the RBI** is a **permanent invitee** to the meetings of the board.
● The **Board is required to meet at least twice a year.** **So, statement III is not correct.** ○ **Decisions** requiring approval during board meetings will be taken by a **majority of members** **present and voting.** In the event of a tie, the chairperson — or in his absence, the deputy governor who is a member of the board  — will have a second or casting vote.
● The Board is responsible for the regulation and supervision of all payment systems, including **electronic** **and non-electronic, domestic and cross-border systems. So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** RBI has constituted a 6-member Payments Regulatory Board, replacing the Board for Regulation and Supervision of Payment and Settlement Systems (BPSS)

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Agreement on Fisheries
Subsidies of the World Trade Organization, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It recognises the principle of Special and
Differential Treatment for developing and least developed countries.
II. 	 It excludes aquaculture and inland fisheries
from its scope.
III. 	A Fish Fund has been established to provide
financial assistance exclusively to the least developed countries for implementing the agreement.
IV. 	 India has ratified this agreement.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies** of the World Trade Organization is considered as the first WTO Agreement focusing on the environment, and the first broad and binding multilateral agreement on ocean sustainability. It is the **WTO's first multilateral agreement with environmental sustainability at its** **core.**
● The Agreement was adopted at the **12th Ministerial Conference of the WTO in 2022**, held in **Geneva,** **Switzerland**, as part of the **Geneva Package**, which refers to the **entire set of agreements and decisions** concluded at MC12, including the **Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies**.
● Under **Special and Differential Treatment**, **developing countries and  Least Developed Countries** **(LDCs)** are granted a **two-year transition period** from the Agreement’s entry into force, during which they are **exempt from implementing the specified disciplines**.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● It is aimed at reducing overfishing. It is designed to **help limit the depletion of fish stocks** caused by excessive fishing. It aims to **phase out harmful fisheries subsidies** and enhance the sustainability of marine resources.
○ The WTO Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies prohibits subsidies that contribute to illegal, unregulated and unreported (IUU) fishing, impact overfished stocks, or are associated with fishing in the high seas. ○ It requires countries to reduce subsidies given to fishing fleets and aims to ensure sustainability of wildlife in the world’s seas and oceans. ○ FSA is the **first binding global agreement** aimed at **curbing harmful fisheries subsidies** that contribute to overcapacity and unsustainable fishing practices.
● This Agreement applies to subsidies to marine wild capture fishing and fishing related activities at high sea. **Aquaculture and inland fisheries are excluded from the scope** of this Agreement.  **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● **WTO Fish Fund has been set up to provide developing economies and least-developed countries** **(LDCs) with technical assistance and capacity-building** needed to implement the new obligations and manage their own fisheries more sustainably. **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ Article 7 of the Agreement on Fisheries Subsidies provides for the creation of the WTO Fisheries Funding Mechanism for targeted technical assistance and capacity-building activities to help
developing and least-developed country members implement the Agreement. On 8 November 2022, the mechanism - known as the WTO Fish Fund - became operational.
