● The **majority of the Brahmanical establishments and the Buddhist ones** can be attributed to the Rashtrakuta times which indicate the religious tolerance of the contemporary period. **The Jaina caves** **post-date the Rashtrakutas** as indicated by the style of execution and fragmentary inscriptions.
o This region was under the control of the **Kalyani Chalukyas and the Yadavas of Deogiri** (Daulatabad) during this period. The patronage towards Jainism under the Yadavas is also known by the findings of several sculptures of Jaina faith from Daulatabad. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **So, only two of the above given statements are correct.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Archaeologist Literary contribution
I. - Alexander Cunningham
The Bhilsa Topes
II. - Alfred Foucher The Monuments of Sanchi
III. - Colin Mackenzie A Forgotten Empire: Vijayanagar
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sir Alexander Cunningham (1814-1893)** was a British army engineer and the **first Director-General** **of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI)**, renowned for his pioneering work in Indian archaeology. In 1837 Cunningham excavated at Sarnath, outside Varanasi, one of the most sacred Buddhist shrines, and carefully prepared drawings of the sculptures.
o In 1850 he excavated Sanchi, site of some of the oldest surviving buildings in India. In addition to a study of the temple architecture of Kashmir (1848) and a work on Ladakh (1854), he published: **The Bhilsa Topes (1854)**, the first serious attempt to trace Buddhist history through its architectural remains. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Alfred Foucher** (1865–1952), was a **French Indologist and specialist in Buddhist archaeology.** He identified many of the Jataka tales (stories of the Buddha's previous lives) depicted in the gateways of Sanchi. He is famous for his "**Aniconic" Theory**: which states that in early Buddhist art (like Sanchi), the Buddha was never shown in human form but was represented by symbols like the Bodhi tree, Empty Throne, or Wheel of Law.
o **In 1914 John Marshall and Alfred Foucher published the book: The Monuments of Sanchi.** **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Colonel Colin Mackenzie, the first Surveyor General of India, played a crucial role in rediscovering** **the ruins of the Vijayanagara Empire at Hampi in 1800. Later, Robert Sewell (1845–1925), who** **served as a magistrate in the Madras Presidency during the colonial period, documented its history** **in his book A Forgotten Empire: Vijayanagar, published in 1900. So, pair III is not correctly** **matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore**, **option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** 2-day Mahabodhi Mahotsav is being held at the Great Stupa at Sanchi in Madhya Pradesh.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
In 1922, Mahatma Gandhi was arrested on charges
of sedition for:

options.b: Articles in Young India, particularly “Tampering with Loyalty”
options.c:
Speeches at the Indian National Congress
session

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In 1922, Mahatma Gandhi was **arrested on charges of sedition** **under Section 124A of the Indian Penal** **Code**, marking a significant moment in the struggle against colonial rule. The sedition charge was based specifically on **three articles published in his English-language weekly Young India in February** **1922**, particularly the article **“Tampering with Loyalty” (23 February 1922)**.
○ In the above article, Gandhi openly expressed disaffection towards the colonial government, with statements such as, **“to preach disaffection towards the existing system of Government has**
**become almost a passion with me”**, which the prosecution cited as evidence of inciting hatred and disloyalty. During the trial, Gandhi pleaded guilty, defended the moral legitimacy of his position, and criticized Section 124A as a tool designed to suppress the liberty of citizens.
● The co-accused was **Shankarlal G. Banker**, the publisher of Young India. Gandhi and Banker were **arrested together on 10 March 1922 from Sabarmati Ashram** and tried in the case Rex Imperator v. M.K. Gandhi. Banker was sentenced separately, while Gandhi **received six years of imprisonment on** **18 March 1922**, reflecting the colonial government’s strict measures against writings deemed seditious. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Modern History

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Bengal British India Society,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 It was established by members of the Young
Bengal movement with the support of George Thompson.
II. 	 It functioned as a platform in India for
radical advocacy against social and religious orthodoxies.
III. 	It actively participated in the Revolt of 1857.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Bengal British India Society** was established in **1843** by members of the **Young Bengal movement**, a group of radical students and intellectuals inspired by **Henry Derozio** at Hindu College. The Society’s formation was supported by the British abolitionist **George Thompson**, reflecting its early engagement with ideas of social reform and justice. Its founding members aimed to address issues of social inequality, promote liberal ideas, and provide a platform for debating progressive concepts in colonial India. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● The Society functioned as an important **platform for radical advocacy** against prevailing **social and** **religious orthodoxies**. Its members encouraged public debates, promoted education, and championed causes such as equality and the welfare of all classes. These activities were consistent with the broader vision of the Young Bengal movement, which emphasized reason, liberty, and challenging entrenched norms. **So, statement II is correct**.
● However, the Bengal British India Society did **not actively participate in the 1857 Indian Rebellion**. While it engaged in social and political reform, its focus was on spreading liberal ideas and shaping public opinion rather than direct military or revolutionary action against British authority. **So, statement III is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Modern History

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
The abolition of 'cowrie' currency and the
requirement of revenue payment in silver were the primary economic triggers for which early anti British resistance?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Paika Rebellion of 1817** in Odisha was one of the earliest armed resistances **against the British** **East India Company.** The Paiks, hereditary landed militia serving the Raja of Khurda, lost their power and prestige after the **British conquest of Odisha in 1803** and the dethronement of the Raja. Economic grievances such as the **extortionist land revenue policies**, **abolition of cowrie currency**, **requirement** **to pay taxes in silver**, and rising salt prices caused widespread resentment among Paiks, zamindars, and peasants, becoming the **primary triggers of the revolt**.
● The rebellion was led by **Bakshi Jagabandhu Bidyadhar**, with support from **Mukunda Deva, the last** **Raja of Khurda**, and local zamindars. Initially successful, the Paikas forced Company forces to retreat, but by 1818 the rebellion was brutally suppressed. Jagabandhu evaded capture for years, surrendering under negotiated terms in 1825. Despite repression, the uprising led to **remissions of arrears, reduced** **assessments, suspension of estate sales, and a new settlement on fixed tenures**, reflecting its impact on governance and marking it as a key early anti-British resistance. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct .**
**Relevance:** Modern History

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Decision Tree and Random Forest, sometimes
seen in the news, are related to:

options.b:
Machine learning algorithms for data analysis
and predictions

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Decision Tree and Random Forest are** **machine learning (ML) algorithms** used for analyzing data and making predictions. A **Decision Tree** uses a tree-like structure where each node represents a decision based on input features, leading to an outcome at the leaf nodes. **Random Forest** is an ensemble technique that combines multiple decision trees to improve prediction accuracy and reduce overfitting, making it more reliable for complex datasets. These algorithms are **discriminative models**, meaning they learn patterns in data to predict outcomes rather than generating new data. They are not related to cryptography, satellite weather models, or genetic modification techniques.
● In practical applications, these ML models are increasingly used to provide **data-driven guidance to** **farmers**, helping them choose the right crops to grow in different seasons. For instance, studies in India have shown that Random Forest models, particularly when combined with **lag variables** (past data points), can accurately recommend high-yield and profitable crops by analyzing environmental, economic, and market data. Such systems help small farmers make informed decisions, improve agricultural productivity, and build resilience against climate change.
● This integration of machine learning with agriculture demonstrates the broader potential of AI to support sustainable and efficient farming practices. By combining environmental and economic inputs, Random Forest-based crop recommendation systems serve as a practical tool bridging sophisticated analytical capabilities with real-world agricultural needs, highlighting how ML algorithms are transforming traditional decision-making processes. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
With reference to Gopalhari Deshmukh ‘Lokahitawadi’, consider the following statements:
I. 	 He served as a judge under the British
administration while contributing to social reform through his writings.
II. 	 He advocated for the reorganization of Indian
society based on rational principles and secular values.
III. 	He was the founder of the periodical Sudharak,
which primarily spoke against untouchability.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Gopalhari Deshmukh**, popularly known by his pen name **‘Lokahitawadi’**, served as a **judge under the** **British administration** while simultaneously emerging as an influential social reformer through his writings, particularly in the weekly **Prabhakar.** His dual role reflects the characteristic nineteenth-century reform tradition where officials also engaged in intellectual and social critique. **So, statement I is correct.**
● He  strongly advocated the reorganisation of Indian society on **rational, modern, humanistic, and** **secular principles**. He criticised Hindu orthodoxy, opposed the caste system, and asserted that religion must change if it obstructed social reform, highlighting his rationalist outlook. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The periodical **Sudharak** was founded and edited by **Gopal Ganesh Agarkar** after he parted ways ideologically with Lokmanya Tilak. The journal became a platform for advocating social equality, women’s education, and the primacy of reason over religious orthodoxy. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Modern History
